title: 2014 NFL season

infobox.value: [University of Phoenix Stadium](/wiki/University_of_Phoenix_Stadium "University of Phoenix Stadium"), [Glendale](/wiki/Glendale,_Arizona "Glendale, Arizona"), [Arizona](/wiki/Arizona "Arizona")

was the 95th season in the history of the [National Football League](/wiki/National_Football_League "National Football League") (NFL) and the 49th of the [Super Bowl](/wiki/Super_Bowl "Super Bowl") era. The season began on Thursday, September 4, 2014, with the [annual kickoff game](/wiki/National_Football_League_Kickoff_game "National Football League Kickoff game") featuring the defending [Super Bowl XLVIII](/wiki/Super_Bowl_XLVIII "Super Bowl XLVIII") champion [Seattle Seahawks](/wiki/2014_Seattle_Seahawks_season "2014 Seattle Seahawks season") hosting the [Green Bay Packers](/wiki/2014_Green_Bay_Packers_season "2014 Green Bay Packers season"), which resulted with the Seahawks winning. The season concluded with [Super Bowl XLIX](/wiki/Super_Bowl_XLIX "Super Bowl XLIX"), the league's [championship game](/wiki/Super_Bowl "Super Bowl"), on Sunday, February 1, 2015, at [University of Phoenix Stadium](/wiki/University_of_Phoenix_Stadium "University of Phoenix Stadium") in [Glendale](/wiki/Glendale,_Arizona "Glendale, Arizona"), [Arizona](/wiki/Arizona "Arizona"), with the [New England Patriots](/wiki/2014_New_England_Patriots_season "2014 New England Patriots season") defeating the Seahawks 28–24, in one of the closest games in Super Bowl history.

**NFC teams: [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/01/Yellow_ffff00_pog.svg/20px-Yellow_ffff00_pog.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Yellow_ffff00_pog.svg) West, [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d7/Blue_pog.svg/20px-Blue_pog.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Blue_pog.svg) North, [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0c/Red_pog.svg/20px-Red_pog.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Red_pog.svg) South, [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1a/White_pog.svg/20px-White_pog.svg.png)](/wiki/File:White_pog.svg) East**

The **2014 NFL season** was the 95th season in the history of the [National Football League](/wiki/National_Football_League "National Football League") (NFL) and the 49th of the [Super Bowl](/wiki/Super_Bowl "Super Bowl") era. The season began on Thursday, September 4, 2014, with the [annual kickoff game](/wiki/National_Football_League_Kickoff_game "National Football League Kickoff game") featuring the defending [Super Bowl XLVIII](/wiki/Super_Bowl_XLVIII "Super Bowl XLVIII") champion [Seattle Seahawks](/wiki/2014_Seattle_Seahawks_season "2014 Seattle Seahawks season") hosting the [Green Bay Packers](/wiki/2014_Green_Bay_Packers_season "2014 Green Bay Packers season"), which resulted with the Seahawks winning. The season concluded with [Super Bowl XLIX](/wiki/Super_Bowl_XLIX "Super Bowl XLIX"), the league's [championship game](/wiki/Super_Bowl "Super Bowl"), on Sunday, February 1, 2015, at [University of Phoenix Stadium](/wiki/University_of_Phoenix_Stadium "University of Phoenix Stadium") in [Glendale](/wiki/Glendale,_Arizona "Glendale, Arizona"), [Arizona](/wiki/Arizona "Arizona"), with the [New England Patriots](/wiki/2014_New_England_Patriots_season "2014 New England Patriots season") defeating the Seahawks 28–24, in one of the closest games in Super Bowl history.

Some notable events of the season include every team losing at least 4 games, the [Carolina Panthers](/wiki/2014_Carolina_Panthers_season "2014 Carolina Panthers season") going to the playoffs despite having a losing record, the NFC Championship Game where the Seahawks would make a comeback against the Packers, and Super Bowl XLIX where the Patriots would intercept the ball at the one yard line and win the game.

## Player movement

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2014_NFL_season&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Player movement")]

The 2014 league year began at 4 pm [EST](/wiki/Eastern_Time_Zone "Eastern Time Zone") on March 11,[[1]](#cite_note-2014Calendar-1) which marked the start of the league's [free agency](/wiki/National_Football_League#Free_agency "National Football League") period.[[2]](#cite_note-2014FreeAgentList-2) The per-team [salary cap](/wiki/NFL_salary_cap "NFL salary cap") was set at [US$](/wiki/US$ "US$")133 million,[[3]](#cite_note-FreeAgencyQuestions-3) a $10 million increase from the [previous year](/wiki/2013_NFL_season "2013 NFL season").[[4]](#cite_note-4) The so-called "legal [tampering](/wiki/Tampering_(sport) "Tampering (sport)")" period[[5]](#cite_note-5) during which time [agents](/wiki/Sports_agents "Sports agents") representing prospective unrestricted free agent players (though not the players themselves) were allowed to have contact with team representatives with the purpose of determining a player's market value and to begin contract negotiations, began at noon ([EST](/wiki/Eastern_Time_Zone "Eastern Time Zone")) on March 8.[[3]](#cite_note-FreeAgencyQuestions-3)

### Free agency

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2014_NFL_season&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Free agency")]

A total of 471 players were eligible for some form of free agency at the beginning of the free agency period. In addition, a number of highly paid players were released after the start of the league year to allow their teams to regain space under the salary cap. Among the high-profile players who changed teams via free agency were:[[6]](#cite_note-6)

* Quarterbacks [Josh McCown](/wiki/Josh_McCown "Josh McCown") (Chicago to Tampa Bay), [Mark Sanchez](/wiki/Mark_Sanchez "Mark Sanchez") (New York Jets to Philadelphia) and [Michael Vick](/wiki/Michael_Vick "Michael Vick") (Philadelphia to New York Jets)
* Running backs [LeGarrette Blount](/wiki/LeGarrette_Blount "LeGarrette Blount") (New England to Pittsburgh), [Donald Brown](/wiki/Donald_Brown_(running_back) "Donald Brown (running back)") (Indianapolis to San Diego), [Toby Gerhart](/wiki/Toby_Gerhart "Toby Gerhart") (Minnesota to Jacksonville), [Chris Johnson](/wiki/Chris_Johnson_(running_back) "Chris Johnson (running back)") (Tennessee to New York Jets), [Maurice Jones-Drew](/wiki/Maurice_Jones-Drew "Maurice Jones-Drew") (Jacksonville to Oakland) and [Ben Tate](/wiki/Ben_Tate "Ben Tate") (Houston to Cleveland)
* Wide receivers [Kenny Britt](/wiki/Kenny_Britt "Kenny Britt") (Tennessee to St. Louis), [Eric Decker](/wiki/Eric_Decker "Eric Decker") (Denver to New York Jets), [Devin Hester](/wiki/Devin_Hester "Devin Hester") (Chicago to Atlanta), [DeSean Jackson](/wiki/DeSean_Jackson "DeSean Jackson") (Philadelphia to Washington), [James Jones](/wiki/James_Jones_(wide_receiver) "James Jones (wide receiver)") (Green Bay to Oakland), [Hakeem Nicks](/wiki/Hakeem_Nicks "Hakeem Nicks") (New York Giants to Indianapolis), [Andre Roberts](/wiki/Andre_Roberts_(American_football) "Andre Roberts (American football)") (Arizona to Washington), [Emmanuel Sanders](/wiki/Emmanuel_Sanders "Emmanuel Sanders") (Pittsburgh to Denver), [Steve Smith](/wiki/Steve_Smith_(wide_receiver,_born_1979) "Steve Smith (wide receiver, born 1979)") (Carolina to Baltimore) and [Golden Tate](/wiki/Golden_Tate "Golden Tate") (Seattle to Detroit)
* Tight ends [Owen Daniels](/wiki/Owen_Daniels_(American_football) "Owen Daniels (American football)") (Houston to Baltimore) and [Brandon Myers](/wiki/Brandon_Myers "Brandon Myers") (New York Giants to Tampa Bay)
* Offensive tackles [Branden Albert](/wiki/Branden_Albert "Branden Albert") (Kansas City to Miami), [Austin Howard](/wiki/Austin_Howard "Austin Howard") (New York Jets to Oakland), [Michael Oher](/wiki/Michael_Oher "Michael Oher") (Baltimore to Tennessee) and [Jared Veldheer](/wiki/Jared_Veldheer "Jared Veldheer") (Oakland to Arizona)
* Guards [Zane Beadles](/wiki/Zane_Beadles "Zane Beadles") (Denver to Jacksonville), [Shawn Lauvao](/wiki/Shawn_Lauvao "Shawn Lauvao") (Cleveland to Washington) and [Geoff Schwartz](/wiki/Geoff_Schwartz "Geoff Schwartz") (Kansas City to New York Giants)
* Defensive tackles [Jason Hatcher](/wiki/Jason_Hatcher "Jason Hatcher") (Dallas to Washington), [Arthur Jones](/wiki/Arthur_Jones_(American_football) "Arthur Jones (American football)") (Baltimore to Indianapolis), [Linval Joseph](/wiki/Linval_Joseph "Linval Joseph") (New York Giants to Minnesota) and [Paul Soliai](/wiki/Paul_Soliai "Paul Soliai") (Miami to Atlanta)
* Defensive ends [Jared Allen](/wiki/Jared_Allen "Jared Allen") (Minnesota to Chicago), [Lamarr Houston](/wiki/Lamarr_Houston "Lamarr Houston") (Oakland to Chicago), [Tyson Jackson](/wiki/Tyson_Jackson "Tyson Jackson") (Kansas City to Atlanta), [Michael Johnson](/wiki/Michael_Johnson_(defensive_end) "Michael Johnson (defensive end)") (Cincinnati to Tampa Bay), [Julius Peppers](/wiki/Julius_Peppers "Julius Peppers") (Chicago to Green Bay), [Antonio Smith](/wiki/Antonio_Smith_(defensive_end) "Antonio Smith (defensive end)") (Houston to Oakland), [Justin Tuck](/wiki/Justin_Tuck "Justin Tuck") (New York Giants to Oakland), [DeMarcus Ware](/wiki/DeMarcus_Ware "DeMarcus Ware") (Dallas to Denver) and [Willie Young](/wiki/Willie_Young_(defensive_end) "Willie Young (defensive end)") (Detroit to Chicago)
* Linebackers [Karlos Dansby](/wiki/Karlos_Dansby "Karlos Dansby") (Arizona to Cleveland), [D'Qwell Jackson](/wiki/D%27Qwell_Jackson "D'Qwell Jackson") (Cleveland to Indianapolis), [Wesley Woodyard](/wiki/Wesley_Woodyard "Wesley Woodyard") (Denver to Tennessee) and [LaMarr Woodley](/wiki/LaMarr_Woodley "LaMarr Woodley") (Pittsburgh to Oakland)
* Cornerbacks [Brandon Flowers](/wiki/Brandon_Flowers_(American_football) "Brandon Flowers (American football)") (Kansas City to San Diego), [Corey Graham](/wiki/Corey_Graham "Corey Graham") (Baltimore to Buffalo), [Captain Munnerlyn](/wiki/Captain_Munnerlyn "Captain Munnerlyn") (Carolina to Minnesota), [Darrelle Revis](/wiki/Darrelle_Revis "Darrelle Revis") (Tampa Bay to New England), [Dominique Rodgers-Cromartie](/wiki/Dominique_Rodgers-Cromartie "Dominique Rodgers-Cromartie") (Denver to New York Giants), [Aqib Talib](/wiki/Aqib_Talib "Aqib Talib") (New England to Denver) and [Alterraun Verner](/wiki/Alterraun_Verner "Alterraun Verner") (Tennessee to Tampa Bay)
* Safeties [Antoine Bethea](/wiki/Antoine_Bethea "Antoine Bethea") (Indianapolis to San Francisco), [Jairus Byrd](/wiki/Jairus_Byrd "Jairus Byrd") (Buffalo to New Orleans), [T. J. Ward](/wiki/T._J._Ward "T. J. Ward") (Cleveland to Denver) and [Donte Whitner](/wiki/Donte_Whitner "Donte Whitner") (San Francisco to Cleveland)

Four players were assigned the [non-exclusive franchise tag](/wiki/Franchise_tag#Types_of_franchise_tag "Franchise tag") by their teams, which ensured that the team would receive compensation were the player to sign a contract with another team. These players were defensive end [Greg Hardy](/wiki/Greg_Hardy "Greg Hardy") ([Panthers](/wiki/2014_Carolina_Panthers_season "2014 Carolina Panthers season")), tight end [Jimmy Graham](/wiki/Jimmy_Graham "Jimmy Graham") ([Saints](/wiki/2014_New_Orleans_Saints_season "2014 New Orleans Saints season")), placekicker [Nick Folk](/wiki/Nick_Folk "Nick Folk") ([Jets](/wiki/2014_New_York_Jets_season "2014 New York Jets season")) and linebacker [Brian Orakpo](/wiki/Brian_Orakpo "Brian Orakpo") ([Redskins](/wiki/2014_Washington_Redskins_season "2014 Washington Redskins season")).[[7]](#cite_note-FranchisePlayers-7) Two other teams used the [transition tag](/wiki/Franchise_tag#Types_of_franchise_tag "Franchise tag"), which offers the player's current team a chance to match offers from other franchises and also guarantees draft pick compensation (at a lesser level than the franchise tag) if a tagged player signs elsewhere. Players given the transition tag were [Jason Worilds](/wiki/Jason_Worilds "Jason Worilds") ([Steelers](/wiki/2014_Pittsburgh_Steelers_season "2014 Pittsburgh Steelers season")) and [Alex Mack](/wiki/Alex_Mack "Alex Mack") ([Browns](/wiki/2014_Cleveland_Browns_season "2014 Cleveland Browns season")). Mack signed a five-year, $42 million [offer sheet](/wiki/Offer_sheet "Offer sheet") with the [Jacksonville Jaguars](/wiki/2014_Jacksonville_Jaguars_season "2014 Jacksonville Jaguars season") which included $26 million in guaranteed money and a player option to void the contract after two seasons. The Browns matched the offer and retained Mack who became the league's highest paid [center](/wiki/Center_(gridiron_football) "Center (gridiron football)").[[8]](#cite_note-8)

One [restricted free agent](/wiki/Exclusive-rights_free_agent "Exclusive-rights free agent") switched teams in 2014: wide receiver [Andrew Hawkins](/wiki/Andrew_Hawkins "Andrew Hawkins") of the [Bengals](/wiki/2013_Cincinnati_Bengals_season "2013 Cincinnati Bengals season") was signed by the [Browns](/wiki/2014_Cleveland_Browns_season "2014 Cleveland Browns season").[[9]](#cite_note-2014RFAs-9) Restricted free agents are players with three or fewer seasons in the league whose contracts have expired. Teams may tender contract offers which allow them to match offers from other teams (i.e. the player's current team gets "right of first refusal") and may trigger draft pick compensation to be received from the signing team.[[10]](#cite_note-CBA-10) Hawkins was tendered at the minimum level, which means the Bengals would not receive any draft compensation.[[11]](#cite_note-11) The Browns signed him to a $13.6 million, four-year offer, which the Bengals declined to match.[[12]](#cite_note-12) [Saints](/wiki/2013_New_Orleans_Saints_season "2013 New Orleans Saints season") safety [Rafael Bush](/wiki/Rafael_Bush "Rafael Bush") signed an offer from the [Falcons](/wiki/2014_Atlanta_Falcons_season "2014 Atlanta Falcons season"), but the Saints retained Bush by matching the offer.[[9]](#cite_note-2014RFAs-9)[[13]](#cite_note-13)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2014_NFL_season&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Trades")]

The following notable trades were made during the 2014 league year:

* March 11: Jacksonville traded QB [Blaine Gabbert](/wiki/Blaine_Gabbert "Blaine Gabbert") to San Francisco for a sixth-round draft pick[[15]](#cite_note-15)
* March 13: New Orleans traded RB [Darren Sproles](/wiki/Darren_Sproles "Darren Sproles") to Philadelphia for a fifth-round draft pick[[16]](#cite_note-16)
* May 9: Buffalo traded WR [Stevie Johnson](/wiki/Stevie_Johnson "Stevie Johnson") to San Francisco for a fourth-round draft pick in [2015](/wiki/2015_NFL_draft "2015 NFL draft")[[17]](#cite_note-17)
* May 10: Philadelphia traded RB [Bryce Brown](/wiki/Bryce_Brown "Bryce Brown") to Buffalo for a fourth-round draft pick in [2015](/wiki/2015_NFL_draft "2015 NFL draft")[[18]](#cite_note-18)
* August 26: New England traded G [Logan Mankins](/wiki/Logan_Mankins "Logan Mankins") to Tampa Bay for a fourth-round draft pick in [2015](/wiki/2015_NFL_draft "2015 NFL draft")[[19]](#cite_note-19)
* August 31: New England traded QB [Ryan Mallett](/wiki/Ryan_Mallett "Ryan Mallett") to Houston for a sixth-round draft pick in [2015](/wiki/2015_NFL_draft "2015 NFL draft")[[20]](#cite_note-20)
* October 17: Seattle traded WR [Percy Harvin](/wiki/Percy_Harvin "Percy Harvin") to New York Jets for a sixth-round draft pick[[21]](#cite_note-21)
* October 28: Tampa Bay traded LB [Mark Barron](/wiki/Mark_Barron "Mark Barron") to St. Louis for a fourth- and sixth-round picks in [2015](/wiki/2015_NFL_draft "2015 NFL draft")[[22]](#cite_note-22)

### Draft

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2014_NFL_season&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Draft")]

Further information: [2014 NFL draft](/wiki/2014_NFL_draft "2014 NFL draft")

The 2014 NFL draft was held May 8–10, 2014, in [New York City](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City").[[23]](#cite_note-23) The draft process began with the [NFL Scouting Combine](/wiki/NFL_Scouting_Combine "NFL Scouting Combine"), where draft-eligible players were evaluated by team personnel, which was held in [Indianapolis](/wiki/Indianapolis "Indianapolis") on February 19–25.[[1]](#cite_note-2014Calendar-1) The draft included a record number of 98 non-[seniors](/wiki/Senior_(education) "Senior (education)").[[24]](#cite_note-EarlyEntrantsPressRelease-24) The event was delayed roughly two weeks compared to its traditional position on the NFL calendar in late April due to a scheduling conflict at [Radio City Music Hall](/wiki/Radio_City_Music_Hall "Radio City Music Hall"),[[25]](#cite_note-25) which had been the draft venue since [2006](/wiki/2006_NFL_season "2006 NFL season"). In the draft, the [Houston Texans](/wiki/2014_Houston_Texans_season "2014 Houston Texans season") made [University of South Carolina](/wiki/2013_South_Carolina_Gamecocks_football_team "2013 South Carolina Gamecocks football team") defensive end, now outside linebacker [Jadeveon Clowney](/wiki/Jadeveon_Clowney "Jadeveon Clowney") the [first overall selection](/wiki/List_of_first_overall_National_Football_League_draft_picks "List of first overall National Football League draft picks").[[26]](#cite_note-26)

There was discussion leading up to the draft as to the future of the event in New York City, where it had been held since [1965](/wiki/1965_NFL_season "1965 NFL season"). Given the increased interest in the draft over the past decade,[[27]](#cite_note-27) there was a belief that the event may have outgrown [Radio City Music Hall](/wiki/Radio_City_Music_Hall "Radio City Music Hall"), which was the venue for the past nine drafts. The possibility of extending the draft to four days was also being discussed. On October 2, 2014, [Auditorium Theatre](/wiki/Auditorium_Theatre "Auditorium Theatre") in Chicago was announced as the official site for the following year's draft.[[28]](#cite_note-28)

## New referees

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2014_NFL_season&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: New referees")]

[Referees](/wiki/List_of_National_Football_League_officials "List of National Football League officials") [Scott Green](/wiki/Scott_Green_(American_football_official) "Scott Green (American football official)") and [Ron Winter](/wiki/Ron_Winter "Ron Winter") retired after the [2013](/wiki/2013_NFL_season "2013 NFL season") season. [Ron Torbert](/wiki/Ron_Torbert "Ron Torbert"), who spent the past four seasons as a side judge, and [Craig Wrolstad](/wiki/Craig_Wrolstad "Craig Wrolstad"), who spent the past 11 seasons as a field judge, were promoted to referee to replace Green and Winter. On June 25, 2014, the [NFL](/wiki/NFL "NFL") announced [Mike Carey](/wiki/Mike_Carey_(American_football) "Mike Carey (American football)")'s retirement as a referee. Like former director of officiating [Mike Pereira](/wiki/Mike_Pereira "Mike Pereira") for Fox Sports, Carey will become the rules/refereeing analyst for CBS's NFL coverage on the network's [Thursday night](/wiki/Thursday_Night_Football "Thursday Night Football") and Sunday afternoon games. He was replaced by [Brad Allen](/wiki/Brad_Allen "Brad Allen"), who spent the past nine seasons as an [ACC](/wiki/Atlantic_Coast_Conference "Atlantic Coast Conference") referee. He was the first rookie NFL referee since 1962 when Tommy Bell was hired as a referee straight out of the [Southeastern Conference](/wiki/Southeastern_Conference "Southeastern Conference"). (NFL officials normally spend at least their first season in another position than head referee.) In addition to promoting Allen, Torbert, and Wrolstad to the referee position, ten new officials were hired, including Shawn Hochuli, son of referee [Ed Hochuli](/wiki/Ed_Hochuli "Ed Hochuli").[[29]](#cite_note-29)

## Rule changes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2014_NFL_season&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Rule changes")]

The following rule changes were passed for the 2014 NFL season at the owners' meeting on March 26, 2014:[[30]](#cite_note-30)

* Eliminating the referee's timeout after a sack (previously the clock did not stop for a sack only after the [two-minute warning](/wiki/Two-minute_warning "Two-minute warning")).
* Simplify spot of enforcement on defensive fouls committed behind the line of scrimmage to enforce from the previous spot instead of the end of the run or the spot of the foul.
* Raise the height of the goal post from 30 feet to 35 feet. The uprights had been 30 feet high since the [1974 NFL season](/wiki/1974_NFL_season "1974 NFL season").
* Extend the restriction on roll-up blocks to include such blocks from the side as well as from the back.
* "Dunking" the football through the goal post or crossbar (or any other means of using the goal post/crossbar as a prop in touchdown celebrations) is now considered unsportsmanlike conduct (15 yards). This rule was in response to [New Orleans Saints](/wiki/New_Orleans_Saints "New Orleans Saints") tight end [Jimmy Graham](/wiki/Jimmy_Graham "Jimmy Graham")'s tendency to dunk the football after scores. One of Graham's dunks bent the goal posts so much that the game was delayed several minutes in order for the stadium crew to make repairs. In addition, the aforementioned rule change to extend the goal posts will add extra weight, increasing the chances that it could collapse. This celebration was previously "grandfathered" as legal much like the [Lambeau Leap](/wiki/Lambeau_Leap "Lambeau Leap").
* Expand replays to cover recovery of loose balls even if the play is blown dead. This was in response to [San Francisco 49ers](/wiki/San_Francisco_49ers "San Francisco 49ers") defensive player [NaVorro Bowman](/wiki/NaVorro_Bowman "NaVorro Bowman")'s clear recovery of a loose ball and downing by contact in the [2013–14 NFL playoffs](/wiki/2013%E2%80%9314_NFL_playoffs "2013–14 NFL playoffs"). The ball was given to the [Seattle Seahawks](/wiki/Seattle_Seahawks "Seattle Seahawks") after Seahawks running back [Michael Robinson](/wiki/Michael_Robinson_(running_back) "Michael Robinson (running back)") took it away from Bowman after Bowman was forced to let go of the ball due to a severe knee injury. Despite the indisputable video evidence of the recovery by Bowman, the play was not reviewable.
* Connecting the officiating command center to the field-to-booth communication relay, allowing the Referee to communicate with the command center during replay reviews. This was in response to some controversial replay reviews during the 2013 season, as well as league officials observing the [NHL](/wiki/NHL "NHL")'s command center. Unlike the NHL's replay system, however, NFL referees will still make the final decisions instead of the command center.
* Make on-field taunting and use of racial/sexual slurs subject to unsportsmanlike conduct penalties (15 yards).

The league has also instructed game officials to strictly enforce offensive pass interference, defensive holding, and illegal contact.[[31]](#cite_note-31)

A proposal to move the line of scrimmage on the extra point try from the 2-yard line to the 25-yard line to increase their difficulty (a 43-yard try as opposed to the more easily makable 20 yards) was tabled (as was a counterproposal from the [Cincinnati Bengals](/wiki/Cincinnati_Bengals "Cincinnati Bengals") to move it up to the 1-yard line, to encourage more [two-point conversions](/wiki/Two-point_conversion "Two-point conversion")),[[32]](#cite_note-32) but the owners approved an experiment of kicking extra points snapping from the 20-yard line (a 38-yard try) for the first two weeks of the preseason. 94.3% of PATs were made during the two-week experiment, as opposed to a 99.6% success rate all of last season.[[33]](#cite_note-33)

### New sideline technologies

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2014_NFL_season&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: New sideline technologies")]

As part of the league's deal with [Microsoft](/wiki/Microsoft "Microsoft"), coaches will be equipped with [Surface tablets](/wiki/Microsoft_Surface "Microsoft Surface") to transmit images of plays taken from the top of the stadium to the sideline, eliminating the traditional practice of using printed photos and notebooks.[[34]](#cite_note-34)

The seven NFL game officials will wear radio headsets to communicate with each other during games, similar to the systems used by [referees](/wiki/Referee_(association_football) "Referee (association football)") at the [FIFA World Cup](/wiki/FIFA_World_Cup "FIFA World Cup") and other higher levels of [association football](/wiki/Association_football "Association football").[[35]](#cite_note-35) With this technology, the officials will not have to move around the field to talk to each other, saving time.

### Practice squads expanded

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2014_NFL_season&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Practice squads expanded")]

The league and the [players' union](/wiki/National_Football_League_Players_Association "National Football League Players Association") agreed in August to some changes to the [practice squad](/wiki/Practice_squad "Practice squad") rules. Under the new rules, each team will be able to carry up to 10 players on their practice squad, up from eight. Practice squad eligibility was also expanded by increasing the number of games in a season a player must be on the squad in order for that season to count as one of the player's three seasons of eligibility from three games to six games. Finally, each practice squad may include two players who have accrued too much playing time to be eligible for the squad under the previous rules, though these players may have no more than two accrued seasons in the league. The new rules cover the 2014 and 2015 seasons, and will lapse in 2016 absent their extension.[[36]](#cite_note-36)

### New drug policy including HGH testing

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2014_NFL_season&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: New drug policy including HGH testing")]

A new drug policy that had been long delayed was agreed to by the league and the [players' union](/wiki/National_Football_League_Players_Association "National Football League Players Association") in September. The revised policy includes testing for [Human growth hormone](/wiki/Human_growth_hormone "Human growth hormone") (HGH) for the first time. Other significant changes include a higher limit for a positive test for [marijuana](/wiki/Marijuana "Marijuana") as well as the reclassification of failed tests for [amphetamines](/wiki/Amphetamines "Amphetamines") during the off-season as falling under the substance-abuse policy rather than the performance-enhancing drug (PED) policy. The changes were applied retroactively for suspensions handed out during the 2014 league year which meant that some players, including [Wes Welker](/wiki/Wes_Welker "Wes Welker") and [Orlando Scandrick](/wiki/Orlando_Scandrick "Orlando Scandrick"), who had failed tests due to amphetamine usage during the off-season were immediately reinstated despite being previously sentenced to four game bans.[[37]](#cite_note-37)

## 2014 deaths

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2014_NFL_season&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: 2014 deaths")]

### William Clay Ford

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2014_NFL_season&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: William Clay Ford")]

[![refer to caption](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b2/William_Clay_Ford_Sr_1961.jpg/250px-William_Clay_Ford_Sr_1961.jpg)](/wiki/File:William_Clay_Ford_Sr_1961.jpg)

William Clay Ford Sr. in 1961

NFL team owners [William Clay Ford Sr.](/wiki/William_Clay_Ford_Sr. "William Clay Ford Sr.") and [Ralph Wilson](/wiki/Ralph_Wilson "Ralph Wilson") both died in March.

[William Clay Ford Sr.](/wiki/William_Clay_Ford_Sr. "William Clay Ford Sr."), the owner of the [Detroit Lions](/wiki/Detroit_Lions "Detroit Lions"), died March 9, two weeks shy of his 89th birthday.[[38]](#cite_note-FordTribObit-38) Ford purchased the Lions in 1963 and had been the team's president since 1961;[[39]](#cite_note-FordLionsTenure-39) at the time of his death, he was the second-longest tenured owner in the NFL, behind only Buffalo Bills owner Ralph Wilson.[[40]](#cite_note-NFLSuccession-40) The team achieved only a single playoff win (in [1991](/wiki/1991_Detroit_Lions_season "1991 Detroit Lions season")) under Ford's ownership.[[39]](#cite_note-FordLionsTenure-39)

Ownership of the team passed to his widow, the former Martha Firestone. Ford's four children, including team vice-chairman [William Clay Ford Jr.](/wiki/William_Clay_Ford_Jr. "William Clay Ford Jr."), are also involved in running the team.[[40]](#cite_note-NFLSuccession-40)

### Ralph Wilson

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2014_NFL_season&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Ralph Wilson")]

[Buffalo Bills](/wiki/Buffalo_Bills "Buffalo Bills") owner [Ralph Wilson](/wiki/Ralph_Wilson "Ralph Wilson") died at age 95 on March 25. He was the founding owner of the franchise, which began in 1960 in the [American Football League](/wiki/American_Football_League "American Football League") (AFL). Wilson played a central role in the negotiations between the AFL and NFL which eventually led to the [AFL–NFL merger](/wiki/AFL%E2%80%93NFL_merger "AFL–NFL merger") in [1970](/wiki/1970_NFL_season "1970 NFL season"). He was the last remaining NFL owner among the [Foolish Club](/wiki/Foolish_Club "Foolish Club"), as the original eight AFL owners were named. Wilson was inducted into the [Pro Football Hall of Fame](/wiki/Pro_Football_Hall_of_Fame "Pro Football Hall of Fame") in 2009.[[41]](#cite_note-WilsonObit-41) Coincidentally, both Wilson and Lions owner William Clay Ford Sr. died at their respective homes in [Grosse Pointe Shores, Michigan](/wiki/Grosse_Pointe_Shores,_Michigan "Grosse Pointe Shores, Michigan"), within three weeks of each other.

Ownership of the Bills passed to a trust headed by Wilson's widow, the former Mary McLean, and controlled by her and three other team officials. The trust put the team up for sale shortly after Wilson's death.[[42]](#cite_note-42) The bidders for the team included payroll processing magnate [Tom Golisano](/wiki/Tom_Golisano "Tom Golisano"), natural gas tycoon [Terrence Pegula](/wiki/Terrence_Pegula "Terrence Pegula"), future President [Donald Trump](/wiki/Donald_Trump "Donald Trump"), a consortium including former Bills quarterback [Jim Kelly](/wiki/Jim_Kelly "Jim Kelly") and bond manager [Jeffrey Gundlach](/wiki/Jeffrey_Gundlach "Jeffrey Gundlach"), and a consortium of [Jon Bon Jovi](/wiki/Jon_Bon_Jovi "Jon Bon Jovi") and the principals of [Maple Leaf Sports and Entertainment](/wiki/Maple_Leaf_Sports_and_Entertainment "Maple Leaf Sports and Entertainment").[[43]](#cite_note-43)[[44]](#cite_note-44)[[45]](#cite_note-45) Despite a poor economy and attendance declines, the team was not in immediate jeopardy of relocating, mainly because of an ironclad stadium lease signed during Wilson's lifetime that effectively prevented the team from leaving until after the 2019 season and because potential Los Angeles-based interests had been intimidated away from buying the team. The Kelly/Gundlach and Golisano groups stepped aside when the Pegulas offered to buy the team, while Trump, who admittedly was not willing to bid as much as the other bidders, did endorse a [smear campaign](/wiki/Smear_campaign "Smear campaign") against the Bon Jovi/MLSE bid, run by political operative [Michael R. Caputo](/wiki/Michael_R._Caputo "Michael R. Caputo"), without focusing any opposition on the Pegulas.[[46]](#cite_note-46) The team was eventually sold to Pegula for a reported price of 1.4 billion dollars.

In honor of Wilson, the Bills wore a patch bearing his initials on their jerseys throughout the 2014 season.[[47]](#cite_note-47)

### Malcolm Glazer

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2014_NFL_season&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Malcolm Glazer")]

[Tampa Bay Buccaneers](/wiki/Tampa_Bay_Buccaneers "Tampa Bay Buccaneers")' owner [Malcolm Glazer](/wiki/Malcolm_Glazer "Malcolm Glazer") died at age 85 on May 28. Glazer had owned the Buccaneers since [1995](/wiki/1995_Tampa_Bay_Buccaneers_season "1995 Tampa Bay Buccaneers season"), a period which covers basically half of the team's history. The [$](/wiki/United_States_dollar "United States dollar")192 million price Glazer paid set a record at the time for the price of a sports franchise. The franchise was in disarray when Glazer bought it after the death of founding owner [Hugh Culverhouse](/wiki/Hugh_Culverhouse "Hugh Culverhouse"). The Bucs had made the playoffs just three times in their 19 years under Culverhouse, while in the 19 seasons since Glazer took over, they made the playoffs seven times, including winning [Super Bowl XXXVII](/wiki/Super_Bowl_XXXVII "Super Bowl XXXVII") in [2002](/wiki/2002_Tampa_Bay_Buccaneers_season "2002 Tampa Bay Buccaneers season").[[48]](#cite_note-TBTGlazerObit-48)

Glazer's widow, Linda Glazer, and the Glazers' six children continue to own and operate the team.[[49]](#cite_note-49)

### Chuck Noll

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2014_NFL_season&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Chuck Noll")]

Former [Pittsburgh Steelers](/wiki/Pittsburgh_Steelers "Pittsburgh Steelers") head coach [Chuck Noll](/wiki/Chuck_Noll "Chuck Noll") died at age 82 on June 13. Noll was widely credited with building the Steelers' dynasty of the 1970s. He coached the team to four Super Bowl victories. When he was hired in 1969, the Steelers had not won a single title in nearly 40 years. He coached multiple [Hall of Fame](/wiki/Hall_of_Fame "Hall of Fame") players including: [Terry Bradshaw](/wiki/Terry_Bradshaw "Terry Bradshaw"), ["Mean" Joe Greene](/wiki/%22Mean%22_Joe_Greene "\"Mean\" Joe Greene"), [Mike Webster](/wiki/Mike_Webster "Mike Webster"), [Jack Ham](/wiki/Jack_Ham "Jack Ham"), [Jack Lambert](/wiki/Jack_Lambert_(American_football) "Jack Lambert (American football)"), [Lynn Swann](/wiki/Lynn_Swann "Lynn Swann"), [John Stallworth](/wiki/John_Stallworth "John Stallworth"), and [Franco Harris](/wiki/Franco_Harris "Franco Harris"). Noll was a member of the [Pro Football Hall of Fame](/wiki/Pro_Football_Hall_of_Fame "Pro Football Hall of Fame").[[50]](#cite_note-50)

### Earl Morrall

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2014_NFL_season&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Earl Morrall")]

[Earl Morrall](/wiki/Earl_Morrall "Earl Morrall") died on April 25 at the age of 79. He had a 21-year NFL career and was a notable member of the [1972 Miami Dolphins](/wiki/1972_Miami_Dolphins "1972 Miami Dolphins") team that is the only NFL squad to ever complete a [perfect season](/wiki/Perfect_season "Perfect season"). Morrall was the second player selected in the [1956 NFL draft](/wiki/1956_NFL_draft "1956 NFL draft") and went on to play for six different NFL teams. He was named the [league MVP](/wiki/National_Football_League_Most_Valuable_Player_Award "National Football League Most Valuable Player Award") in [1968](/wiki/1968_NFL_season "1968 NFL season") when he led the [Baltimore Colts](/wiki/1968_Baltimore_Colts_season "1968 Baltimore Colts season") to a 13–1 record and the [Super Bowl](/wiki/Super_Bowl_III "Super Bowl III") after replacing an injured [Johnny Unitas](/wiki/Johnny_Unitas "Johnny Unitas"). After joining the Dolphins in 1972 Morrall again proved to be a valuable back-up, replacing [Bob Griese](/wiki/Bob_Griese "Bob Griese") who suffered a broken ankle in the fifth game of the Dolphins immaculate season. Morrall started the final nine regular season games, before giving way to a healed Griese during the [1972–73 NFL playoffs](/wiki/1972%E2%80%9373_NFL_playoffs "1972–73 NFL playoffs"). Morrall was named the [Comeback Player of the Year](/wiki/National_Football_League_Comeback_Player_of_the_Year_Award "National Football League Comeback Player of the Year Award") by [Pro Football Weekly](/wiki/Pro_Football_Weekly "Pro Football Weekly") for his contribution in 1972.[[51]](#cite_note-51)

## Preseason

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2014_NFL_season&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Preseason")]

[Training camps](/wiki/Training_camp_(National_Football_League) "Training camp (National Football League)") for the 2014 season were held in late July through August. Teams may start training camp no earlier than 15 days before the team's first scheduled preseason game.[[1]](#cite_note-2014Calendar-1)

Prior to the start of the regular season, each team played four preseason [exhibition games](/wiki/Exhibition_game "Exhibition game"). The [preseason](/wiki/National_Football_League_preseason "National Football League preseason") schedule got underway with the [Pro Football Hall of Fame Game](/wiki/Pro_Football_Hall_of_Fame_Game "Pro Football Hall of Fame Game") on Sunday evening, August 3. The Hall of Fame game is a traditional part of the annual [Pro Football Hall of Fame](/wiki/Pro_Football_Hall_of_Fame "Pro Football Hall of Fame") induction weekend celebrating new Hall of Fame members. It was played at [Fawcett Stadium](/wiki/Fawcett_Stadium "Fawcett Stadium") which is located adjacent to the Hall of Fame building in [Canton, Ohio](/wiki/Canton,_Ohio "Canton, Ohio"). The game, which was televised in the U.S. on [NBC](/wiki/NFL_on_NBC "NFL on NBC"), featured the [New York Giants](/wiki/2014_New_York_Giants_season "2014 New York Giants season") and [Buffalo Bills](/wiki/2014_Buffalo_Bills_season "2014 Buffalo Bills season"), with the Giants winning 17–13.[[53]](#cite_note-53) Continuing the recent trend of scheduling teams that are associated with former players being inducted into the Hall, the 2014 class included former Giants defensive end [Michael Strahan](/wiki/Michael_Strahan "Michael Strahan") and former Bills wide receiver [Andre Reed](/wiki/Andre_Reed "Andre Reed"). The others who were inducted into the Hall of Fame are linebacker [Derrick Brooks](/wiki/Derrick_Brooks "Derrick Brooks"), punter [Ray Guy](/wiki/Ray_Guy "Ray Guy"), defensive end [Claude Humphrey](/wiki/Claude_Humphrey "Claude Humphrey"), offensive tackle [Walter Jones](/wiki/Walter_Jones_(American_football) "Walter Jones (American football)"), and defensive back [Aeneas Williams](/wiki/Aeneas_Williams "Aeneas Williams").[[54]](#cite_note-2014HOFClass-54) The 65-game preseason schedule wrapped up on Thursday, August 28, a week before the start of the regular season.[[55]](#cite_note-55)

## Regular season

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2014_NFL_season&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Regular season")]

The 2014 [regular season](/wiki/National_Football_League_regular_season "National Football League regular season") featured 256 games played out over a seventeen-week schedule which began on the Thursday night following [Labor Day](/wiki/Labor_Day "Labor Day"). Each of the league's 32 teams played a 16-game schedule with one [bye week](/wiki/Bye_(sports) "Bye (sports)") for each team scheduled between weeks four and twelve. The slate featured seventeen games on [Monday night](/wiki/Monday_Night_Football "Monday Night Football") including a [doubleheader](/wiki/Doubleheader_(television)#National_Football_League "Doubleheader (television)") in the season's opening week. There were seventeen games played on Thursday, including the [National Football League Kickoff game](/wiki/National_Football_League_Kickoff_game "National Football League Kickoff game") in [prime time](/wiki/Prime_time "Prime time") on September 4 and three games on [Thanksgiving Day](/wiki/NFL_on_Thanksgiving_Day "NFL on Thanksgiving Day"). The schedule also included two games played on Saturday, December 20, in the season's sixteenth week. The regular season concluded with a full slate of 16 games on Sunday, December 28, all of which, as it was since [2010](/wiki/2010_NFL_season "2010 NFL season"), were intra-divisional matchups.[[56]](#cite_note-2014ScheduleAnnouncement-56)

### Scheduling formula

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2014_NFL_season&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Scheduling formula")]

Under the NFL's current [scheduling formula](/wiki/NFL_scheduling_formula "NFL scheduling formula"), each team plays each of the other three teams in their own division twice. In addition, a team plays against all four teams in one other division from each conference. The final two games on a team's schedule are against the two teams in the team's own conference in the divisions the team was not set to play who finished the previous season in the same rank in their division (e.g. the team which finished first in its division the previous season would play each other team in their conference that also finished first in its respective division). The pre-set division pairings for 2014 were as follows:

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
| **Intra-conference**  [AFC East](/wiki/AFC_East "AFC East") vs. [AFC West](/wiki/AFC_West "AFC West")  [AFC North](/wiki/AFC_North "AFC North") vs. [AFC South](/wiki/AFC_South "AFC South")  [NFC East](/wiki/NFC_East "NFC East") vs. [NFC West](/wiki/NFC_West "NFC West")  [NFC North](/wiki/NFC_North "NFC North") vs. [NFC South](/wiki/NFC_South "NFC South") | **Inter-conference**  [AFC East](/wiki/AFC_East "AFC East") vs. [NFC North](/wiki/NFC_North "NFC North")  [AFC North](/wiki/AFC_North "AFC North") vs. [NFC South](/wiki/NFC_South "NFC South")  [AFC South](/wiki/AFC_South "AFC South") vs. [NFC East](/wiki/NFC_East "NFC East")  [AFC West](/wiki/AFC_West "AFC West") vs. [NFC West](/wiki/NFC_West "NFC West") |

Highlights of the 2014 schedule include:

* **[International Series](/wiki/NFL_International_Series "NFL International Series")**: Three games were played at [Wembley Stadium](/wiki/Wembley_Stadium "Wembley Stadium") in [London](/wiki/London "London"), [England](/wiki/England "England") in 2014. The [Oakland Raiders](/wiki/2014_Oakland_Raiders_season "2014 Oakland Raiders season") lost to the [Miami Dolphins](/wiki/2014_Miami_Dolphins_season "2014 Miami Dolphins season") 38–14 on September 28, the [Atlanta Falcons](/wiki/2014_Atlanta_Falcons_season "2014 Atlanta Falcons season") lost to the [Detroit Lions](/wiki/2014_Detroit_Lions_season "2014 Detroit Lions season") 22–21 on October 26,[[57]](#cite_note-57) and the [Jacksonville Jaguars](/wiki/2014_Jacksonville_Jaguars_season "2014 Jacksonville Jaguars season") lost to the [Dallas Cowboys](/wiki/2014_Dallas_Cowboys_season "2014 Dallas Cowboys season") 31–17 on November 9, in the second of four consecutive appearances for the Jaguars in the International Series.[[58]](#cite_note-58)[[59]](#cite_note-59) [CBS](/wiki/NFL_on_CBS "NFL on CBS") televised the Miami–Oakland game, while [Fox](/wiki/NFL_on_Fox "NFL on Fox") televised the Dallas–Jacksonville and Detroit–Atlanta contests.[[60]](#cite_note-60)
* **[Thanksgiving Day games](/wiki/NFL_on_Thanksgiving_Day "NFL on Thanksgiving Day")**: These games occurred on Thursday, November 27, 2014. The [Detroit Lions](/wiki/2014_Detroit_Lions_season "2014 Detroit Lions season") hosted the [Chicago Bears](/wiki/2014_Chicago_Bears_season "2014 Chicago Bears season") at 12:30 p.m. ET, and aired on CBS, while the [Dallas Cowboys](/wiki/2014_Dallas_Cowboys_season "2014 Dallas Cowboys season") hosted the [Philadelphia Eagles](/wiki/2014_Philadelphia_Eagles_season "2014 Philadelphia Eagles season") at 4:30 p.m. ET (Dallas last hosted Philadelphia on Thanksgiving 25 years prior in what became known as the [Bounty Bowl](/wiki/Bounty_Bowl "Bounty Bowl")), and aired on Fox. The prime-time NBC game, featured the [San Francisco 49ers](/wiki/2014_San_Francisco_49ers_season "2014 San Francisco 49ers season") hosting the [Seattle Seahawks](/wiki/2014_Seattle_Seahawks_season "2014 Seattle Seahawks season") in a rematch of the previous year's [NFC Championship Game](/wiki/NFC_Championship_Game "NFC Championship Game"), was featured at 8:30 p.m. ET. For the first time ever, no AFC teams appeared on Thanksgiving.

On March 4, 2014, the [Buffalo Bills](/wiki/Buffalo_Bills "Buffalo Bills")' official radio flagship ([WGR](/wiki/WGR "WGR")) confirmed that the [Bills Toronto Series](/wiki/Bills_Toronto_Series "Bills Toronto Series") would not take place in 2014[[61]](#cite_note-61) and that the future of the series, which was otherwise scheduled to run through the 2017 season,[[62]](#cite_note-62) was not yet certain. The series was formally terminated on December 3, 2014.[[63]](#cite_note-63)[[64]](#cite_note-64)

### Changes to flex scheduling

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2014_NFL_season&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Changes to flex scheduling")]

The NFL introduced two major changes to the flexible scheduling procedure. First, the league would now be able to "cross-flex" games between CBS and Fox, enabling CBS to televise NFC away games (for the first time since [1993](/wiki/1993_NFL_season "1993 NFL season")), and Fox to broadcast AFC away games (for the first time since [2011](/wiki/2011_NFL_season "2011 NFL season"), and all-AFC matchups for the first time ever).[[65]](#cite_note-65)[[66]](#cite_note-Newsday_NewTVContract-66) The league could "cross-flex" some of these games before the start of, or during, the season. The first game affected by this "cross-flexing" change was the Week One contest between the [Buffalo Bills](/wiki/2014_Buffalo_Bills_season "2014 Buffalo Bills season") and [Chicago Bears](/wiki/2014_Chicago_Bears_season "2014 Chicago Bears season") at [Soldier Field](/wiki/Soldier_Field "Soldier Field"), which aired on Fox instead of CBS;[[67]](#cite_note-67) all four of the Bills' interconference games (including their Week 5 game against the [Detroit Lions](/wiki/2014_Detroit_Lions_season "2014 Detroit Lions season") at [Ford Field](/wiki/Ford_Field "Ford Field")) aired on Fox, and in Week 6, an all-AFC matchup, again involving the Bills (this time a division rivalry game against the [New England Patriots](/wiki/2014_New_England_Patriots_season "2014 New England Patriots season")) was moved to Fox, exacerbating financial problems at Buffalo's CBS affiliate [WIVB-TV](/wiki/WIVB-TV "WIVB-TV").[[68]](#cite_note-68) An all-NFC matchup between the [Carolina Panthers](/wiki/2014_Carolina_Panthers_season "2014 Carolina Panthers season") and [Seattle Seahawks](/wiki/2014_Seattle_Seahawks_season "2014 Seattle Seahawks season") was given to CBS.[[69]](#cite_note-cbj-nflncbsgames-69) The aforementioned Detroit-Chicago Thanksgiving game was also given to CBS instead of Fox.

On April 23, 2014, the league announced a second major change to the flexible scheduling procedure: games could be flexed into the NBC Sunday Night time slot as soon as Week 5. NBC was allowed to flex up to two games between Weeks 5 and 10, while the same rules applied for the remainder of the season.[[70]](#cite_note-70)

### In-season scheduling changes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2014_NFL_season&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: In-season scheduling changes")]

The following games were moved by way of [flexible scheduling](/wiki/NFL_on_Television#Flexible_scheduling "NFL on Television"), severe weather, or for other reasons:

* **Week 6**: The [New England](/wiki/2014_New_England_Patriots_season "2014 New England Patriots season")–[Buffalo](/wiki/2014_Buffalo_Bills_season "2014 Buffalo Bills season") game was "cross-flexed" from [CBS](/wiki/NFL_on_CBS "NFL on CBS") to [Fox](/wiki/NFL_on_Fox "NFL on Fox"), while the [Chicago](/wiki/2014_Chicago_Bears_season "2014 Chicago Bears season")–[Atlanta](/wiki/2014_Atlanta_Falcons_season "2014 Atlanta Falcons season") game was moved from 1:00 p.m. to 4:25 p.m. [ET](/wiki/Eastern_Time_Zone "Eastern Time Zone") (still on Fox).[[71]](#cite_note-71)
* **Week 8**: The [Seattle](/wiki/2014_Seattle_Seahawks_season "2014 Seattle Seahawks season")–[Carolina](/wiki/2014_Carolina_Panthers_season "2014 Carolina Panthers season") game was "cross-flexed" from Fox to CBS, while the [Houston](/wiki/2014_Houston_Texans_season "2014 Houston Texans season")–[Tennessee](/wiki/2014_Tennessee_Titans_season "2014 Tennessee Titans season") game was "cross-flexed" from CBS to Fox, keeping their 1:00 p.m. ET kickoff times.[[72]](#cite_note-72)
* **Week 11**: The [Minnesota](/wiki/2014_Minnesota_Vikings_season "2014 Minnesota Vikings season")–[Chicago](/wiki/2014_Chicago_Bears_season "2014 Chicago Bears season") game was "cross-flexed" from Fox to CBS, while the [Philadelphia](/wiki/2014_Philadelphia_Eagles_season "2014 Philadelphia Eagles season")–[Green Bay](/wiki/2014_Green_Bay_Packers_season "2014 Green Bay Packers season") game was moved from 1:00 p.m. to 4:25 p.m. ET (still on Fox).[[73]](#cite_note-73)
* **Week 12**: The [New York Jets](/wiki/2014_New_York_Jets_season "2014 New York Jets season")–[Buffalo](/wiki/2014_Buffalo_Bills_season "2014 Buffalo Bills season") game was relocated from Sunday, November 23 at 1:00 p.m. ET to [Ford Field](/wiki/Ford_Field "Ford Field") in [Detroit](/wiki/Detroit "Detroit") on Monday, November 24 at 7:00 p.m. ET due to the ["Knife" lake-effect snowstorm](/wiki/November_2014_Western_New_York_snowstorm "November 2014 Western New York snowstorm") that hit the [Buffalo](/wiki/Buffalo,_New_York "Buffalo, New York") area the previous week. The game aired locally on [WCBS-TV](/wiki/WCBS-TV "WCBS-TV") and [WIVB-TV](/wiki/WIVB-TV "WIVB-TV"), respectively, while the previously scheduled *Monday Night Football* game between the Baltimore and New Orleans aired as scheduled.[[74]](#cite_note-74)
* **Week 14**: The [Indianapolis](/wiki/2014_Indianapolis_Colts_season "2014 Indianapolis Colts season")–[Cleveland](/wiki/2014_Cleveland_Browns_season "2014 Cleveland Browns season") game was "cross-flexed" from CBS to Fox, keeping the same 1:00 p.m. ET kickoff time.[[75]](#cite_note-75)
* **Week 15**: The [Cincinnati](/wiki/2014_Cincinnati_Bengals_season "2014 Cincinnati Bengals season")–[Cleveland](/wiki/2014_Cleveland_Browns_season "2014 Cleveland Browns season") game was "cross-flexed" from CBS to Fox, while the [Minnesota](/wiki/2014_Minnesota_Vikings_season "2014 Minnesota Vikings season")–[Detroit](/wiki/2014_Detroit_Lions_season "2014 Detroit Lions season") game was moved from 1:00 p.m. to 4:25 p.m. ET (still on Fox).[[76]](#cite_note-Wk15Flex-Wk16SatDH-76)
* **Week 16**: On November 30, the league announced the final start times and networks for the two Saturday, December 20 games: the [Philadelphia](/wiki/2014_Philadelphia_Eagles_season "2014 Philadelphia Eagles season")–[Washington](/wiki/2014_Washington_Redskins_season "2014 Washington Redskins season") game was played at 4:25 p.m. ET and aired on NFL Network, and the [San Diego](/wiki/2014_San_Diego_Chargers "2014 San Diego Chargers")–[San Francisco](/wiki/2014_San_Francisco_49ers_season "2014 San Francisco 49ers season") game was held at 8:25 p.m. ET and was broadcast on CBS.[[76]](#cite_note-Wk15Flex-Wk16SatDH-76)
* **Week 17**:
  + The [Cincinnati](/wiki/2014_Cincinnati_Bengals_season "2014 Cincinnati Bengals season")–[Pittsburgh](/wiki/2014_Pittsburgh_Steelers_season "2014 Pittsburgh Steelers season") game, originally scheduled for 1 p.m. ET on CBS, was selected as the final *NBC Sunday Night Football* game, which decided the AFC North champion.[[77]](#cite_note-77)
  + The [Jacksonville](/wiki/2014_Jacksonville_Jaguars_season "2014 Jacksonville Jaguars season")–[Houston](/wiki/2014_Houston_Texans_season "2014 Houston Texans season") game, originally scheduled for 1 p.m. ET on CBS, was "cross-flexed" to Fox (keeping the same kickoff time).[[78]](#cite_note-78)
  + The [Carolina](/wiki/2014_Carolina_Panthers_season "2014 Carolina Panthers season")–[Atlanta](/wiki/2014_Atlanta_Falcons_season "2014 Atlanta Falcons season") game, which decided the NFC South champion, originally scheduled for 1 p.m. ET on Fox, was "cross-flexed" to 4:25 p.m. ET on CBS.[[79]](#cite_note-79)
  + The [Detroit](/wiki/2014_Detroit_Lions_season "2014 Detroit Lions season")–[Green Bay](/wiki/2014_Green_Bay_Packers_season "2014 Green Bay Packers season") game, which decided the NFC North champion, originally scheduled for 1 p.m. ET on Fox, was moved to 4:25 p.m. ET (still on Fox).[[80]](#cite_note-80)

## Postseason

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2014_NFL_season&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Postseason")]

Main article: [2014–15 NFL playoffs](/wiki/2014%E2%80%9315_NFL_playoffs "2014–15 NFL playoffs")

The wild card round was played on January 3–4, 2015. Divisional round games were played on January 10–11. Conference Championship Games were played on January 18 with the [NFC Championship Game](/wiki/NFC_Championship_Game "NFC Championship Game") at 3:00 pm [EST](/wiki/Eastern_Time_Zone_(North_America) "Eastern Time Zone (North America)") on Fox and the [AFC Championship Game](/wiki/AFC_Championship_Game "AFC Championship Game") following at 6:30 pm EST on CBS.[[56]](#cite_note-2014ScheduleAnnouncement-56)

### Super Bowl XLIX

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2014_NFL_season&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Super Bowl XLIX")]

Main article: [Super Bowl XLIX](/wiki/Super_Bowl_XLIX "Super Bowl XLIX")

Super Bowl XLIX, the 49th contesting of the [Super Bowl](/wiki/Super_Bowl "Super Bowl"), decided the 2014 NFL champion on February 1, 2015, with the [New England Patriots](/wiki/New_England_Patriots "New England Patriots") defeating the defending [Super Bowl XLVIII](/wiki/Super_Bowl_XLVIII "Super Bowl XLVIII") champions [Seattle Seahawks](/wiki/Seattle_Seahawks "Seattle Seahawks"), who were looking to repeat, by a score of 28–24. With a controversial play call by Seattle's head coach, [Pete Carroll](/wiki/Pete_Carroll "Pete Carroll"), they decided to try to pass the ball in for a touchdown at the 1 yard line, the ball was intercepted by rookie cornerback [Malcolm Butler](/wiki/Malcolm_Butler "Malcolm Butler"). Thus, New England sealed the win. The game took place at [University of Phoenix Stadium](/wiki/University_of_Phoenix_Stadium "University of Phoenix Stadium") in [Glendale](/wiki/Glendale,_Arizona "Glendale, Arizona"), [Arizona](/wiki/Arizona "Arizona").[[84]](#cite_note-SuperBowlAnnouncement-95) It was televised in the U.S. by [NBC](/wiki/NFL_on_NBC "NFL on NBC") with kickoff around 6:30 p.m. EST.[[85]](#cite_note-96)

## Pro Bowl

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2014_NFL_season&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: Pro Bowl")]

Main article: [2015 Pro Bowl](/wiki/2015_Pro_Bowl "2015 Pro Bowl")

The Pro Bowl is the league's [all-star game](/wiki/All-star_game "All-star game"). On April 9, 2014, the NFL announced that the [2015 Pro Bowl](/wiki/2015_Pro_Bowl "2015 Pro Bowl") would be played the week prior to the Super Bowl at [University of Phoenix Stadium](/wiki/University_of_Phoenix_Stadium "University of Phoenix Stadium") in [Glendale, Arizona](/wiki/Glendale,_Arizona "Glendale, Arizona"). It was broadcast in the U.S. by ESPN on Sunday, January 25, 2015. The [draft](/wiki/Draft_(sports) "Draft (sports)") format that debuted in the [2014 Pro Bowl](/wiki/2014_Pro_Bowl "2014 Pro Bowl") continued with two former players, [Cris Carter](/wiki/Cris_Carter "Cris Carter") and [Michael Irvin](/wiki/Michael_Irvin "Michael Irvin"), drafting their players from a select list voted by the country, and without regard to league conference. Cleveland Browns CB [Joe Haden](/wiki/Joe_Haden "Joe Haden") was named a captain of one of the Pro Bowl rosters alongside RB DeMarco Murray of Dallas on January 15. DE J. J. Watt of Houston and WR Antonio Brown of Pittsburgh were named opposite roster captains.[[86]](#cite_note-ProBowlAnnouncement-97)
