[Michael Sam](/wiki/Michael_Sam "Michael Sam")

[Michael Sam](/wiki/Michael_Sam "Michael Sam"), an [All-America](/wiki/All-America "All-America") defensive lineman from [Missouri](/wiki/2013_Missouri_Tigers_football_team "2013 Missouri Tigers football team") who was named the [SEC](/wiki/Southeastern_Conference "Southeastern Conference") Defensive Player of the Year in 2013, announced in February that he is [gay](/wiki/Gay "Gay").[[87]](#cite_note-98) Sam was selected by the [St. Louis Rams](/wiki/2014_St._Louis_Rams_season "2014 St. Louis Rams season") in the seventh round of the 2014 draft with [the 249th overall selection](/wiki/2014_NFL_draft#Pick_249 "2014 NFL draft").[[88]](#cite_note-99) He became the first openly gay draftee to be selected in the [NFL draft](/wiki/NFL_draft "NFL draft") and if he made an NFL roster, he would become the first openly gay player in the NFL.

Sam, the Rams and the NFL were publicly congratulated upon Sam's selection by U.S. president [Barack Obama](/wiki/Barack_Obama "Barack Obama") who called Sam's selection "an important step forward today in our Nation's journey."[[89]](#cite_note-100) However, Sam stated after the draft that he felt he "should have gone in the top three rounds easily"[[90]](#cite_note-101) and there was speculation that his announcement of his sexuality caused him to fall in the draft.[[91]](#cite_note-102) Sam was the first ever SEC Defensive Player of the Year to not be selected in the first round of the draft.[[92]](#cite_note-MichaelSamNYTBelson-103) On the other hand, Sam's performance at the [NFL Scouting Combine](/wiki/NFL_Scouting_Combine "NFL Scouting Combine") was widely judged as "mediocre" and at least one draft analyst assessed his odds of making an NFL roster as only "slightly better than average."[[92]](#cite_note-MichaelSamNYTBelson-103)

[ESPN](/wiki/ESPN "ESPN") aired Sam's reaction to being drafted which included an embrace and celebratory kiss with his partner. [Miami Dolphins](/wiki/2014_Miami_Dolphins_season "2014 Miami Dolphins season") safety [Don Jones](/wiki/Don_Jones_(American_football) "Don Jones (American football)") tweeted a negative reaction to the display, which caused the Dolphins to fine him and ban him from participation in off-season team activities until he undergoes [sensitivity training](/wiki/Sensitivity_training "Sensitivity training").[[93]](#cite_note-104)

On August 30, Sam was cut by the Rams a few days before the regular season was to start. On September 3, the Dallas Cowboys added Sam to their practice squad; he was released from the practice squad on October 21.[[94]](#cite_note-105)

### Lawsuits by NFL cheerleaders

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2014_NFL_season&action=edit&section=31 "Edit section: Lawsuits by NFL cheerleaders")]

During the offseason, a major headline was many current and former [cheerleaders](/wiki/National_Football_League_Cheerleading "National Football League Cheerleading") filing lawsuits against their respective NFL teams for unfair labor practices. Members of the [Buffalo Jills](/wiki/Buffalo_Jills "Buffalo Jills"), [Cincinnati Ben–Gals](/wiki/Cincinnati_Ben%E2%80%93Gals "Cincinnati Ben–Gals"), [Jets Flight Crew](/wiki/Jets_Flight_Crew "Jets Flight Crew"), [Oakland Raiderettes](/wiki/Oakland_Raiderettes "Oakland Raiderettes"), and [Tampa Bay Buccaneers Cheerleaders](/wiki/Tampa_Bay_Buccaneers_Cheerleaders "Tampa Bay Buccaneers Cheerleaders") all filed lawsuits against their respective teams (or, in the case of the Jills, the non-profit organization that runs the Jills) for violating their respective state laws for labor practices, stating that they were required to meet certain appearance standards in both weight and make-up (at the expense of the cheerleaders) and attend mandatory unpaid practices, while being paid very little for each game, and in some cases, only receiving a lump sum payment after the season.[[95]](#cite_note-106) One lawsuit by a Raiderette, who had a young child at home and was prompted by her husband to initiate the lawsuit, was ruled as a seasonal job in federal court and thus not subject to [minimum wage](/wiki/Minimum_wage_in_the_United_States "Minimum wage in the United States") laws, but still was pending in a [California](/wiki/California "California") state court and that state's more stringent labor laws.[[96]](#cite_note-107) Most controversial was the Jills' "Jiggle Test" that became public knowledge.[[97]](#cite_note-108) The Jills suspended operations indefinitely in response to the lawsuit.[[98]](#cite_note-109) It has been speculated that all NFL teams might drop their cheerleading squads in response to the lawsuits instead of paying their squads accordingly despite having the financial means to do so,[[99]](#cite_note-110) though the [Cleveland Browns](/wiki/Cleveland_Browns "Cleveland Browns") are considering adding cheerleaders to the team.[[100]](#cite_note-111)

### Washington Redskins name controversy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2014_NFL_season&action=edit&section=32 "Edit section: Washington Redskins name controversy")]

Main article: [Washington Redskins name controversy](/wiki/Washington_Redskins_name_controversy "Washington Redskins name controversy")

On June 18, 2014, the [United States Patent and Trademark Office](/wiki/United_States_Patent_and_Trademark_Office "United States Patent and Trademark Office"), in a 2–1 decision, invalidated some of the [trademark](/wiki/Trademark "Trademark") protections of the [Washington Redskins](/wiki/Washington_Redskins "Washington Redskins"), stating that the use of the team name "Redskins" constituted an [ethnic slur](/wiki/Ethnic_slur "Ethnic slur").[[101]](#cite_note-112) The Redskins are not required to change their name. However, if the decision is upheld on appeal (the team intends to appeal and has no intention of changing the team name), they will not be able to prevent [counterfeiters](/wiki/Counterfeit_consumer_goods "Counterfeit consumer goods") from manufacturing certain knockoff Redskins' apparel.

With the [Minnesota Vikings](/wiki/Minnesota_Vikings "Minnesota Vikings") playing their first of two consecutive seasons at the [University of Minnesota](/wiki/University_of_Minnesota "University of Minnesota")'s [TCF Bank Stadium](/wiki/TCF_Bank_Stadium "TCF Bank Stadium") ([see below](#Stadiums)), the university asked the Vikings to keep the Washington team's name from being used in printed materials or uttered by the game announcer at the stadium. The college also requested that the Redskins wear their throwback jerseys without the team name and logo when they visited the Vikings on November 2. University officials said that the use of the Redskins name at their stadium violates the institution's affirmative action, diversity and equal opportunity policy. Lester Bagley, the Vikings' executive vice president of public affairs, said that the team is still deciding how it will handle the college's request.[[102]](#cite_note-113)[[103]](#cite_note-114)

### Broncos' owner Pat Bowlen relinquishes control of team

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2014_NFL_season&action=edit&section=33 "Edit section: Broncos' owner Pat Bowlen relinquishes control of team")]

On July 23, [Pat Bowlen](/wiki/Pat_Bowlen "Pat Bowlen"), the [Denver Broncos](/wiki/Denver_Broncos "Denver Broncos")' owner since [1984](/wiki/1984_NFL_season "1984 NFL season"), relinquished control of the team due to his battle with [Alzheimer's disease](/wiki/Alzheimer%27s_disease "Alzheimer's disease"). Bowlen had been privately battling the disease since 2009 after experiencing short-term memory loss, and had since taken a reduced role with the team, resulting in team president [Joe Ellis](/wiki/Joe_Ellis "Joe Ellis") and executive vice president/general manager [John Elway](/wiki/John_Elway "John Elway") making team decisions. Ellis and Elway assumed full control of the team, though Bowlen's long-term plan was for one of his seven children to run the team in the future, preventing the Broncos from being put up for sale.[[104]](#cite_note-PatBowlen-115)[[105]](#cite_note-116)

### Discipline for off-field incidents

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2014_NFL_season&action=edit&section=34 "Edit section: Discipline for off-field incidents")]

### Colts' owner Jim Irsay guilty of OWI; suspended 6 games

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2014_NFL_season&action=edit&section=35 "Edit section: Colts' owner Jim Irsay guilty of OWI; suspended 6 games")]

[Indianapolis Colts](/wiki/Indianapolis_Colts "Indianapolis Colts") owner [Jim Irsay](/wiki/Jim_Irsay "Jim Irsay") was arrested on March 16 near his home in [Carmel, Indiana](/wiki/Carmel,_Indiana "Carmel, Indiana"), on suspicion of [driving under the influence](/wiki/Driving_under_the_influence "Driving under the influence") (DUI) after failing field sobriety tests. A search of Irsay's vehicle revealed "numerous prescription medication bottles containing pills" as well as almost $30,000 in cash. Preliminary charges of DUI and four counts of possession of a controlled substance were filed against Irsay, who was jailed overnight.[[106]](#cite_note-117) Irsay entered a [rehabilitation](/wiki/Drug_rehabilitation "Drug rehabilitation") facility shortly after his release from jail.[[107]](#cite_note-118) Irsay had undergone treatment for prescription drug addiction previously in the early 2000s.[[108]](#cite_note-119) Irsay returned to the Colts shortly before the [draft](/wiki/2014_NFL_draft "2014 NFL draft") in May.[[109]](#cite_note-120)

In late May, Irsay was charged with two [misdemeanor](/wiki/Misdemeanor "Misdemeanor") counts in the incident: [operating a vehicle while intoxicated](/wiki/Driving_under_the_influence "Driving under the influence") and operating a vehicle with a [controlled substance](/wiki/Controlled_substance "Controlled substance") in the body. Prosecutors allege Irsay was under the influence of [oxycodone](/wiki/Oxycodone "Oxycodone") or [hydrocodone](/wiki/Hydrocodone "Hydrocodone"), both of which are prescription opioid narcotic pain medications.[[110]](#cite_note-121)

Irsay pleaded guilty on September 2 to one count of [operating a vehicle while intoxicated](/wiki/Driving_under_the_influence "Driving under the influence") (OWI), which is a [Class C misdemeanor](/wiki/Class_A_Misdemeanor "Class A Misdemeanor") in Indiana. He was sentenced to one year of [probation](/wiki/Probation "Probation") during which he is forbidden from consuming or possessing alcohol and his driving privileges were revoked for a period of one year and 40 days. He will be subject to random drug testing as a condition of his probation. In addition, the judge stipulated that any subsequent OWI charge in a five-year period will be treated as a [felony](/wiki/Felony "Felony"). The NFL banned Irsay from any contact with his team for six games and also levied a fine of $500,000. He was also forbidden from doing any media interviews or making comments related to the team on social media during the suspension. It was expected that Irsay's daughter, Carlie Irsay-Gordon, would assume control of the team during his absence.[[111]](#cite_note-122)

### Ray Rice domestic violence suspension

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2014_NFL_season&action=edit&section=36 "Edit section: Ray Rice domestic violence suspension")]

On February 15, 2014, Baltimore Ravens running back [Ray Rice](/wiki/Ray_Rice "Ray Rice") and his fiancée Janay Palmer were both arrested for assault after a physical altercation that took place at [Revel Casino](/wiki/Revel_Casino "Revel Casino") in [Atlantic City, New Jersey](/wiki/Atlantic_City,_New_Jersey "Atlantic City, New Jersey"). Celebrity news website [TMZ](/wiki/TMZ "TMZ") posted a video of Rice dragging Palmer's body out of an elevator at the casino.[[112]](#cite_note-123) The Ravens issued a statement following TMZ's release of the video, calling Rice's [domestic violence](/wiki/Domestic_violence "Domestic violence") arrest a "serious matter".[[113]](#cite_note-124)

On March 27, 2014, a grand jury indicted Rice on charges of third-degree [aggravated assault](/wiki/Aggravated_assault "Aggravated assault"), which could carry a jail sentence of three to five years in prison and a fine of up to $15,000. Charges against Palmer were dropped.[[114]](#cite_note-125) Rice and Palmer were married the day after his indictment.[[115]](#cite_note-126) Rice pleaded guilty to one count of third degree [aggravated assault](/wiki/Aggravated_assault "Aggravated assault") and was accepted into a pretrial intervention program for first-time offenders on May 20.[[116]](#cite_note-127) Under the terms of the plea deal, the charges would be dropped and expunged from Rice's record if he meets the conditions imposed by the judge for the next 12 months.[[117]](#cite_note-128)

Rice was suspended by the NFL for the first two games of the 2014 NFL season on July 25.[[118]](#cite_note-129) On September 8, [TMZ](/wiki/TMZ "TMZ") released footage from a camera inside the elevator in which the assault took place. The video appears to show Rice punching Palmer in the face causing Palmer to immediately fall to the ground, perhaps striking her head on the elevator's handrail on the way to the floor, and leaving her motionless. Within hours of the video's release, the Baltimore Ravens terminated Rice's contract. Shortly thereafter, Goodell announced that Rice had been suspended from the league indefinitely.[[119]](#cite_note-130)[[120]](#cite_note-131)

Rice, along with the [players' union](/wiki/NFLPA "NFLPA"), appealed the indefinite suspension on the grounds that a player cannot be disciplined twice for the same incident. The union requested that Goodell recuse himself from hearing the appeal (as he normally would under the league's personal conduct policy) since he will be a witness in the proceedings.[[121]](#cite_note-132)

In late November, Rice was reinstated. It's reported that there are four teams looking into picking up Rice for the 2015 season. Two of those teams have been identified as the New Orleans Saints and Indianapolis Colts.[[122]](#cite_note-133)

### New policy on domestic violence instituted by league

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2014_NFL_season&action=edit&section=37 "Edit section: New policy on domestic violence instituted by league")]

As a result of widespread criticism of the two-game suspension handed down in the Ray Rice case, which was considered too lenient by many commentators,[[123]](#cite_note-134) the NFL announced a new policy on dealing with domestic violence on August 28. Under the new policy the first offense of domestic violence would be punishable by a minimum six-game suspension without pay; a second offense would result in a "lifetime" ban from the league. These rules will apply to all league personnel, including executives and owners, not just players. A person who receives a "lifetime" ban would be eligible to petition the league for reinstatement after one year. The penalty for the first offense could be increased by a number of factors including a previous incident prior to joining the league, the use of a weapon, an act committed against a pregnant woman and the presence of a child.

### Reaction to the new domestic violence policy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2014_NFL_season&action=edit&section=38 "Edit section: Reaction to the new domestic violence policy")]

According to an [Associated Press](/wiki/Associated_Press "Associated Press") article on [ESPN](/wiki/ESPN "ESPN"), the players' union has questioned why the NFL's domestic violence training and education program "treats all players as perpetrators." In a memo sent to the [NFL Players Association](/wiki/NFL_Players_Association "NFL Players Association") members on Thursday by Executive Director [DeMaurice Smith](/wiki/DeMaurice_Smith "DeMaurice Smith") and obtained Friday by the Associated Press, the union also said the plan, "doesn't build a positive consensus to warning signs." Smith and union special counsel Teri Patterson described two meetings this month with the league in which an [NFLPA](/wiki/NFLPA "NFLPA") commission was briefed on the league's approach to educating players, coaches, executives, owners and NFL personnel about domestic violence. He wrote that a "good overview of domestic violence, sexual assault and child abuse" was presented. But "it did not address larger issues of violence in and outside of the home."

The NFL said of the "perpetrators" claim: "Nothing could be further from the truth. The presentation expressly recognizes that people in the NFL are often falsely portrayed and that the actions of a few damage the reputations of many." "What the program teaches is that everyone can and should be part of the solution," the league statement said. The union memo also said the "NFL's presentation doesn't focus on follow-ups and providing continuous resources at the clubs to address potentially violent situations as well as preventing them." The NFL's educational program was shown to the AP on Oct. 7, and it included information from a memo sent to the 32 clubs on Sept. 18 that pointed out local resources available to all team personnel and their families. That document indicated a plan was in place to provide those resources and follow-ups for those who need it. The union memo to the players also said the NFL presentation "doesn't include any psychological information about the type of behavior that could lead to acts of violence or warning signs of negative behavior, but instead seemed to focus almost entirely on what happens after a violent incident has been committed."

The league's plan calls for experts who work in the psychological space to offer a research perspective of societal issues, recognized that these are intimate crimes that impact people in many ways. The program calls for each club to have such experts available to the teams, or what NFL calls "the entire club family." That can include a clinician, human resource workers, player engagement executives, security personnel and a mental health professional who works with the club. The union added that although the league indicated that the trainers for this educational program will be experts, the NFL did not list any specific names, titles or relevant backgrounds of the people they intend to utilize for the training. Previously, the NFL announced an advisory group that includes authorities in the domestic violence area such as Tony Porter, Beth E. Richie, Rita Smith, Jane Randel and Lisa Friel. Another NFLPA observation was: "Too much reliance was placed on using former players to participate in the training. While one former players possess the right qualifications and experience to train personnel on these issues, the league's inability to articulate who these players are raises concerns that call into question the effectiveness of the training." Many of the player ambassadors, as the NFL calls them, have personal testimonies around these issues and might be helpful, but they would not deliver the education program. The union added: "The league stated that at each presentation, they will distribute information on suggested local (team city/state specific) resources for domestic violence and sexual assault prevention specialists, licensed club mental health clinicians, club human resource directors and director of Player Engagement.

The NFLPA commission members recommended that a broader net of resources be included, such as faith-based counselors and male-focused community organizations, etc. The NFL did not provide any explanation as to why one resource was chosen over another or how those resources would be specifically integrated into the workplace, if at all." In response to the union memo, the NFL said: "We were pleased to meet with the union and are working to incorporate their suggestions into the presentations is the start of a process of education that will continue in future years."[[124]](#cite_note-135)[[125]](#cite_note-136)

### Adrian Peterson child abuse arrest and benched

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2014_NFL_season&action=edit&section=39 "Edit section: Adrian Peterson child abuse arrest and benched")]

Minnesota Vikings running back [Adrian Peterson](/wiki/Adrian_Peterson "Adrian Peterson") was indicted by a Houston grand jury on a [felony](/wiki/Felony "Felony") charge of injury to a child on September 12. The charge stemmed from a beating with a tree branch, or [switch](/wiki/Switch_(corporal_punishment) "Switch (corporal punishment)"), which Peterson allegedly administered to his 4-year-old son as a disciplinary measure.[[126]](#cite_note-137) Peterson turned himself in for arrest following the indictment.

The Vikings announced following the arrest that Peterson would be deactivated and would not play in the team's game that weekend. The following Monday the team announced that Peterson would be allowed to rejoin the team. The team reversed direction two days later and placed Peterson on the inactive list pending resolution of the charges.[[127]](#cite_note-138)

### Greg Hardy found guilty of domestic violence; placed on leave

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2014_NFL_season&action=edit&section=40 "Edit section: Greg Hardy found guilty of domestic violence; placed on leave")]

Carolina Panthers defensive end [Greg Hardy](/wiki/Greg_Hardy "Greg Hardy") was arrested on charges of domestic violence after an altercation with his girlfriend which took place on May 13. In July, Hardy was found guilty of assaulting a female and communicating threats and sentenced to 18 months of probation in a trial before a district judge. Hardy immediately requested a trial by jury which under [North Carolina](/wiki/North_Carolina "North Carolina")'s legal system means that the terms of the sentencing are put on hold pending the jury trial.[[128]](#cite_note-139)

Because the legal process had not yet played out, Hardy was not disciplined immediately by either the league or the Panthers and he was allowed to play in the team's first game. However, following the outcry surrounding the Ray Rice and Adrian Peterson situations, Hardy was de-activated for the Panther's second game and was subsequently placed on the inactive list until the charges are adjudicated. Hardy continued to get paid, but was not allowed to have any contact with the Panthers organization.[[129]](#cite_note-140)

### Possible franchise relocations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2014_NFL_season&action=edit&section=41 "Edit section: Possible franchise relocations")]

#### Buffalo Bills

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2014_NFL_season&action=edit&section=42 "Edit section: Buffalo Bills")]

See also: [National Football League in Toronto](/wiki/National_Football_League_in_Toronto "National Football League in Toronto")

In July 2014 it was reported that [Jon Bon Jovi](/wiki/Jon_Bon_Jovi "Jon Bon Jovi") and the principals of [Maple Leaf Sports & Entertainment](/wiki/Maple_Leaf_Sports_%26_Entertainment "Maple Leaf Sports & Entertainment") (a consortium between [Bills Toronto Series](/wiki/Bills_Toronto_Series "Bills Toronto Series") lessee [Edward Rogers III](/wiki/Edward_Rogers_III "Edward Rogers III") and [Larry Tanenbaum](/wiki/Larry_Tanenbaum "Larry Tanenbaum")) had joined forces as equal partners to bid on the Buffalo Bills, with Bon Jovi the proposed controlling owner.[[130]](#cite_note-equalstakes-141) It was also reported that the group had conducted a feasibility study in early 2013 on the construction of an NFL stadium in Toronto.[[131]](#cite_note-feasibility-142) Following protests by Buffalo area fans against the Toronto group, including radio stations banning Bon Jovi's music,[[132]](#cite_note-143) Bon Jovi wrote a public letter to Bills fans saying that the group's objective was to "make the Bills successful in Buffalo" and committing to work with all levels of government "to identify the best possible site in the Buffalo area for a new stadium",[[133]](#cite_note-144) though it noticeably did not promise to keep the team in Buffalo.[[134]](#cite_note-145) The group claimed it had plans to meet with developers in the Buffalo region to discuss the construction of a new stadium.[[135]](#cite_note-vital-146)[[136]](#cite_note-only3-147) However, there was widespread skepticism about the sincerity of the group's pledge to keep the team in Buffalo,[[135]](#cite_note-vital-146) with a sports franchise relocation expert quoted as saying that if they did intend to move the club, "I would suggest never saying that publicly" due to the legal implications of selling the team to a group planning on relocating it.[[137]](#cite_note-148)

The Toronto group was one of four known to have submitted a preliminary bid for the franchise. The other groups, which intend to keep the club in Buffalo, were: eventual winner [Terrence Pegula](/wiki/Terrence_Pegula "Terrence Pegula"), owner of the [Buffalo Sabres](/wiki/Buffalo_Sabres "Buffalo Sabres"); [Donald Trump](/wiki/Donald_Trump "Donald Trump"), who formerly owned the [New Jersey Generals](/wiki/New_Jersey_Generals "New Jersey Generals"); and [Tom Golisano](/wiki/Tom_Golisano "Tom Golisano"), former owner of the [Buffalo Sabres](/wiki/Buffalo_Sabres "Buffalo Sabres").[[136]](#cite_note-only3-147)[[138]](#cite_note-149) Trump described his chances of being the successful bidder as "very, very unlikely because I'm not going to do something totally stupid."[[139]](#cite_note-fewnew-150) Other Canadians who explored purchasing the team include [John Bitove](/wiki/John_Bitove "John Bitove"), who was co-founder the [Toronto Raptors](/wiki/Toronto_Raptors "Toronto Raptors"), and the family of [Francesco Aquilini](/wiki/Francesco_Aquilini "Francesco Aquilini"), who owns the [Vancouver Canucks](/wiki/Vancouver_Canucks "Vancouver Canucks"), though it is unknown if they intended to move the team.[[140]](#cite_note-151)[[141]](#cite_note-152) Though Tanenbaum and Rogers had significant wealth, it had been reported that the Toronto group's bid was limited by Bon Jovi's resources and his desire to be the controlling partner; NFL regulations require the controlling partner to have a 30% equity stake.[[142]](#cite_note-153) [Los Angeles-based interests](/wiki/History_of_the_National_Football_League_in_Los_Angeles "History of the National Football League in Los Angeles") largely stayed away from the Bills, citing concerns over the stadium's lease and the fear that politicians will place intense scrutiny on any person who attempts to move the Bills out of Buffalo; multibillionaire [Eli Broad](/wiki/Eli_Broad "Eli Broad") declined to place a bid on the team for those reasons.[[143]](#cite_note-graham81414-154)

The sale was completed on September 9, to the Pegulas for a reported amount of 1.4 billion dollars which made the Bills purchase the most expensive in league history. The sale was done in time for the Pegulas to be unanimously approved at the NFL's owners meeting on October 6–8.[[139]](#cite_note-fewnew-150) [Terry Pegula](/wiki/Terry_Pegula "Terry Pegula"), owns the [Buffalo Sabres](/wiki/Buffalo_Sabres "Buffalo Sabres") and development projects in the Buffalo area. The Pegulas intend to keep the team in Western New York for the long term.

Any possible relocation had one of the other bidders won (or if Pegula resells the team to anyone in the future) could not happen under the terms of the Bills' current lease on [Ralph Wilson Stadium](/wiki/Ralph_Wilson_Stadium "Ralph Wilson Stadium") until the end of the 2019 season and would have, at least on paper, required league endorsement (whether the league could, in and of itself, stop a relocation is unclear; [Al Davis](/wiki/Al_Davis "Al Davis") [moved](/wiki/History_of_the_Oakland_Raiders#Los_Angeles_era_(1982–1994) "History of the Oakland Raiders") the [Oakland Raiders](/wiki/Oakland_Raiders "Oakland Raiders") to Los Angeles in 1982 against the league's wishes). And a relocation fee, which had been speculated to be $100–$200 million, could be charged by the NFL,[[144]](#cite_note-sixyears-155)[[145]](#cite_note-inquestion-156)[[146]](#cite_note-atleast2020-157)[[147]](#cite_note-strewn-158)[[148]](#cite_note-trust-159)[[149]](#cite_note-niagarabills-160) though this could be offset by an increase in franchise value in a larger market.[[148]](#cite_note-trust-159)[[150]](#cite_note-atrisk-161) Goodell has said the two votes would be held separately.[[151]](#cite_note-goodellstadium-162) Of the owners who made their position known, [Jerry Jones](/wiki/Jerry_Jones "Jerry Jones") was believed to have been in favor of the Bills moving to Toronto, while [John Mara](/wiki/John_Mara "John Mara"), [Robert Wood Johnson IV](/wiki/Robert_Wood_Johnson_IV "Robert Wood Johnson IV"), the [Green Bay Packers Board of Directors](/wiki/Green_Bay_Packers_Board_of_Directors "Green Bay Packers Board of Directors"), and [Shahid Khan](/wiki/Shahid_Khan "Shahid Khan") indicated they would have most likely opposed moving the team.[[152]](#cite_note-163)

#### Oakland Raiders

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2014_NFL_season&action=edit&section=43 "Edit section: Oakland Raiders")]

See also: [History of the National Football League in Los Angeles](/wiki/History_of_the_National_Football_League_in_Los_Angeles "History of the National Football League in Los Angeles")

On July 29, 2014, reports surfaced that the [Oakland Raiders](/wiki/Oakland_Raiders "Oakland Raiders") were considering [relocating](/wiki/National_Football_League_franchise_moves_and_mergers "National Football League franchise moves and mergers") to [San Antonio](/wiki/San_Antonio "San Antonio") in [2015](/wiki/2015_NFL_season "2015 NFL season") after owner [Mark Davis](/wiki/Mark_Davis_(American_football) "Mark Davis (American football)") met with San Antonio civic leaders the week before at the encouragement of former Raider [Cliff Branch](/wiki/Cliff_Branch "Cliff Branch"), which Davis was in town to visit for a local ceremony for Branch. The Raiders themselves had acknowledged Davis being in San Antonio for the event for Branch before news broke about a possible relocation, but wouldn't confirm nor deny that Davis also mentioned being there discussing moving his team east.[[153]](#cite_note-Raiders-164) Among the two existing NFL teams in [Texas](/wiki/Texas "Texas"), [Houston Texans](/wiki/Houston_Texans "Houston Texans")' owner [Bob McNair](/wiki/Bob_McNair "Bob McNair") and [Dallas Cowboys](/wiki/Dallas_Cowboys "Dallas Cowboys")' owner [Jerry Jones](/wiki/Jerry_Jones "Jerry Jones")—the latter of which has San Antonio as part of his territorial rights and previously voiced support of an NFL team moving there when the [New Orleans Saints](/wiki/New_Orleans_Saints "New Orleans Saints") [temporarily played in San Antonio in 2005](/wiki/Effect_of_Hurricane_Katrina_on_the_New_Orleans_Saints "Effect of Hurricane Katrina on the New Orleans Saints") due to [damages to the Superdome following Hurricane Katrina](/wiki/Effect_of_Hurricane_Katrina_on_the_Louisiana_Superdome "Effect of Hurricane Katrina on the Louisiana Superdome")—both favor an NFL team playing in San Antonio.[[154]](#cite_note-165)

Though San Antonio is a smaller market than the [San Francisco Bay Area](/wiki/San_Francisco_Bay_Area "San Francisco Bay Area"), the Raiders wouldn't be sharing the market with another NFL team, and would only compete with the [NBA](/wiki/NBA "NBA")'s [San Antonio Spurs](/wiki/San_Antonio_Spurs "San Antonio Spurs") among major sports teams. Additionally, the Raiders would use the [Alamodome](/wiki/Alamodome "Alamodome") as a temporary home until an NFL-specific stadium could be constructed.[[153]](#cite_note-Raiders-164) The team's lease at the [O.co Coliseum](/wiki/O.co_Coliseum "O.co Coliseum") was expiring after the 2014 season.[[155]](#cite_note-166)

On September 3, 2014, the city of [Oakland](/wiki/Oakland "Oakland") reached a tentative deal to build a new football stadium in Oakland, which would result in the Coliseum being demolished; Davis did not respond to the proposal, which would also force the [Oakland Athletics](/wiki/Oakland_Athletics "Oakland Athletics") to build a new stadium of their own (which they have yet to agree to do), while [Alameda County](/wiki/Alameda_County "Alameda County") (co-owners of the current stadium) indicated they would probably not support the plan.[[156]](#cite_note-San_Francisco_Chronicle-167) Davis, in the meantime, continued to negotiate with San Antonio officials and had team officials scout the Alamodome to determine if it would be suitable for the NFL.[[157]](#cite_note-168)

#### St. Louis Rams

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2014_NFL_season&action=edit&section=44 "Edit section: St. Louis Rams")]

Main article: [The Dome at America's Center § The loss of the Rams (2012–2016)](/wiki/The_Dome_at_America%27s_Center#The_loss_of_the_Rams_(2012–2016) "The Dome at America's Center")

The Rams and the St. Louis CVC began negotiating deals to get the Rams home stadium, the [Edward Jones Dome](/wiki/Edward_Jones_Dome "Edward Jones Dome") into the top 25 percent of stadiums in the league (i.e., top eight teams of the thirty two NFL teams in reference to luxury boxes, amenities and overall fan experience). Under the terms of the lease agreement, the St. Louis CVC was required to make modifications to the Edward Jones Dome in 2005. However, then-owner, [Georgia Frontiere](/wiki/Georgia_Frontiere "Georgia Frontiere"), waived the provision in exchange for cash that served as a penalty for the city's noncompliance. The City of St. Louis, in subsequent years, made changes to the score board and increased the natural lighting by replacing panels with windows, although the overall feel remained dark. The minor renovations which totaled about $70 million did not bring the stadium within the specifications required under the lease agreement. On February 1, 2013, an Arbitrator (3 panel) selected to preside over the arbitration process found that the Edward Jones Dome was not in the top 25% of all NFL venues as required under the terms of the lease agreement between the Rams and the CVC. The Arbitrator (3 panel) further found that the estimated $700 million in proposed renovations by the Rams was not unreasonable given the terms of the lease agreement. Finally, the City of St. Louis was ordered to pay the Rams attorneys' fees which totaled a reported $2 million.

Publicly, city, county and state officials expressed no interest in providing further funding to the Edward Jones Dome in light of those entities, as well as taxpayers, continuing to owe approximately $300 million more on that facility. As such, if a resolution is not reached by the end of the 2014–2015 NFL season and the City of St. Louis remains non-compliant in its obligations under the lease agreement, the Rams would be free to nullify their lease and relocate.

On January 31, 2014, both the *Los Angeles Times* and the *St. Louis Post-Dispatch* reported that Rams owner [Stan Kroenke](/wiki/Stan_Kroenke "Stan Kroenke") purchased 60 acres of land adjacent to the [Forum](/wiki/The_Forum_(Inglewood) "The Forum (Inglewood)") in [Inglewood](/wiki/Inglewood,_California "Inglewood, California"), [Los Angeles County](/wiki/Los_Angeles_County "Los Angeles County"), [California](/wiki/California "California"). It was, by the most conservative estimates, sufficient land on which an NFL-proper stadium may be constructed. The purchase price was rumored to have been between US$90–100 million. Commissioner Roger Goodell represented that Mr. Kroenke informed the league of the purchase. As an NFL owner, any purchase of land in which a potential stadium could be built must be disclosed to the league. This development further fueled rumors that the Rams intend to return its management and football operations to Southern California. The land was initially targeted for a Walmart Supercenter but [Walmart](/wiki/Walmart "Walmart") could not get the necessary permits to build the center. Kroenke is married to [Ann Walton Kroenke](/wiki/Ann_Walton_Kroenke "Ann Walton Kroenke") who is a member of the [Walton family](/wiki/Walton_family "Walton family") and many of Kroenke's real estate deals have involved Walmart properties.[[158]](#cite_note-169)[[159]](#cite_note-170)

## Records, milestones, and notable statistics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2014_NFL_season&action=edit&section=45 "Edit section: Records, milestones, and notable statistics")]

### Week 1

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2014_NFL_season&action=edit&section=46 "Edit section: Week 1")]

* [Peyton Manning](/wiki/Peyton_Manning "Peyton Manning") defeated his former team, the [Indianapolis Colts](/wiki/Indianapolis_Colts "Indianapolis Colts"), and in doing so became the second quarterback to defeat all 32 current NFL teams, joining [Brett Favre](/wiki/Brett_Favre "Brett Favre").[[160]](#cite_note-171)
* [Allen Hurns](/wiki/Allen_Hurns "Allen Hurns") became the first player in NFL history to have two TDs receiving in the first quarter in his NFL debut.[[161]](#cite_note-172)
* In Week 1, quarterbacks completed 64.3 percent of their passes, an NFL record.[[162]](#cite_note-173)

### Week 2

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2014_NFL_season&action=edit&section=47 "Edit section: Week 2")]

* [Bill Belichick](/wiki/Bill_Belichick "Bill Belichick") became the sixth coach in NFL history to reach 200 regular-season wins.[[163]](#cite_note-174)
* The [Philadelphia Eagles](/wiki/2014_Philadelphia_Eagles_season "2014 Philadelphia Eagles season") became the first team in NFL history to start the season 2–0 after trailing by at least 14 points at halftime in each of their first two games.

### Week 3

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2014_NFL_season&action=edit&section=48 "Edit section: Week 3")]

* [Devin Hester](/wiki/Devin_Hester "Devin Hester") set an NFL record with 20 total returns for touchdowns. The previous record was held by [Deion Sanders](/wiki/Deion_Sanders "Deion Sanders"), who had 19 touchdowns.[[164]](#cite_note-175)
* The [Philadelphia Eagles](/wiki/2014_Philadelphia_Eagles_season "2014 Philadelphia Eagles season") became the first NFL team to start a season 3–0 after trailing by 10-plus points in each game.[[165]](#cite_note-176)
* [Peyton Manning](/wiki/Peyton_Manning "Peyton Manning") threw his 100th touchdown pass in his 35th game with the [Denver Broncos](/wiki/Denver_Broncos "Denver Broncos"), becoming the fastest quarterback in NFL history to achieve that feat with a single team. The previous record was held by [Dan Marino](/wiki/Dan_Marino "Dan Marino"), who did so in 44 games with the [Miami Dolphins](/wiki/Miami_Dolphins "Miami Dolphins").[[166]](#cite_note-177)

### Week 4

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2014_NFL_season&action=edit&section=49 "Edit section: Week 4")]

* The [Green Bay Packers](/wiki/2014_Green_Bay_Packers_season "2014 Green Bay Packers season") became the second NFL franchise to record their 700th regular-season victory, joining the [Chicago Bears](/wiki/Chicago_Bears "Chicago Bears").[[167]](#cite_note-Week4_Sunday7-178)
* There were zero punts in the Packers–[Bears](/wiki/2014_Chicago_Bears_season "2014 Chicago Bears season") game, making it the second regular-season game in NFL history with no punts. The other regular-season game without a punt was a [1992 game](/wiki/No_Punt_Game "No Punt Game") between the [Buffalo Bills](/wiki/1992_Buffalo_Bills_season "1992 Buffalo Bills season") and the [San Francisco 49ers](/wiki/1992_San_Francisco_49ers_season "1992 San Francisco 49ers season").[[167]](#cite_note-Week4_Sunday7-178)
* Colts quarterback [Andrew Luck](/wiki/Andrew_Luck "Andrew Luck") became the first player in NFL history to throw for over 370 yards and 4 touchdowns, and have a completion percentage above 70 percent or above in consecutive games.[[168]](#cite_note-179)

### Week 5

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2014_NFL_season&action=edit&section=50 "Edit section: Week 5")]

* The [Cleveland Browns](/wiki/2014_Cleveland_Browns_season "2014 Cleveland Browns season") overcame a 28–3 deficit in their 29–28 win at [Tennessee](/wiki/2014_Tennessee_Titans_season "2014 Tennessee Titans season") which was the largest comeback victory by an away team in NFL history.[[169]](#cite_note-Week5-180)
* [Peyton Manning](/wiki/Peyton_Manning "Peyton Manning") became the second quarterback in NFL history to throw 500 touchdown passes, joining [Brett Favre](/wiki/Brett_Favre "Brett Favre").[[169]](#cite_note-Week5-180)
* [Jason Witten](/wiki/Jason_Witten "Jason Witten") became the third tight-end in NFL history to reach 10,000 career receiving yards, joining [Shannon Sharpe](/wiki/Shannon_Sharpe "Shannon Sharpe") and [Tony Gonzalez](/wiki/Tony_Gonzalez "Tony Gonzalez").[[169]](#cite_note-Week5-180)
* [Tom Brady](/wiki/Tom_Brady "Tom Brady") became the sixth quarterback in NFL history to reach 50,000 career passing yards.[[170]](#cite_note-181)
* [Wes Welker](/wiki/Wes_Welker "Wes Welker") became the NFL's all-time leader in career pass receptions amongst [undrafted](/wiki/Undrafted_free_agent "Undrafted free agent") wide receivers.[[171]](#cite_note-182)
* No team started the season 4–0. This is only the third time that this has happened in the modern era; the others were [1970](/wiki/1970_NFL_season "1970 NFL season") and [2010](/wiki/2010_NFL_season "2010 NFL season").[[172]](#cite_note-183)

### Week 6

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2014_NFL_season&action=edit&section=51 "Edit section: Week 6")]

* The [Chicago Bears](/wiki/2014_Chicago_Bears_season "2014 Chicago Bears season") became the first NFL franchise to reach 750 total wins.[[173]](#cite_note-Week6-184)
* [DeMarco Murray](/wiki/DeMarco_Murray "DeMarco Murray") became the second player in NFL history to rush for at least 100 yards in each of his team's first six games to start a season, joining [Jim Brown](/wiki/Jim_Brown "Jim Brown").[[173]](#cite_note-Week6-184)
* [Julius Thomas](/wiki/Julius_Thomas "Julius Thomas"), with his nine touchdown catches through his team's first five games of the season, tied the NFL record that was set by [Calvin Johnson](/wiki/Calvin_Johnson "Calvin Johnson") in 2011.[[174]](#cite_note-Week6-2-185)
* With a passer rating above 120 for the fifth straight game, [Philip Rivers](/wiki/Philip_Rivers "Philip Rivers") has set an NFL record for most consecutive games above this mark. The previous record was shared by [Johnny Unitas](/wiki/Johnny_Unitas "Johnny Unitas") in 1965 and [Kurt Warner](/wiki/Kurt_Warner "Kurt Warner") in 2009, who each had four.[[174]](#cite_note-Week6-2-185)
* [Joe Flacco](/wiki/Joe_Flacco "Joe Flacco") tied a league record set by [Tommy Kramer](/wiki/Tommy_Kramer "Tommy Kramer") in 1986 by recording 4 touchdown passes in the first quarter of a game.[[175]](#cite_note-186)
* The [Carolina Panthers](/wiki/2014_Carolina_Panthers_season "2014 Carolina Panthers season") and [Cincinnati Bengals](/wiki/2014_Cincinnati_Bengals_season "2014 Cincinnati Bengals season") played to a 37–37 tie. This was the highest scoring tie game since the [1974](/wiki/1974_NFL_season "1974 NFL season") introduction of overtime to the regular season.

### Week 7

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2014_NFL_season&action=edit&section=52 "Edit section: Week 7")]

* [Peyton Manning](/wiki/Peyton_Manning "Peyton Manning") threw his 509th career touchdown pass, setting an NFL record. The previous record was held by [Brett Favre](/wiki/Brett_Favre "Brett Favre"), who had 508.[[176]](#cite_note-187)
* [DeMarco Murray](/wiki/DeMarco_Murray "DeMarco Murray") became the first player in NFL history to rush for at least 100 yards in each of his team's first seven games to start a season, breaking the record he held with [Jim Brown](/wiki/Jim_Brown "Jim Brown"). Murray would go on to extend his record to eight games.[[177]](#cite_note-Week7-188)
* [Reggie Wayne](/wiki/Reggie_Wayne "Reggie Wayne") became the ninth player in NFL history to reach 14,000 receiving yards.[[177]](#cite_note-Week7-188)
* Russell Wilson became the first player in NFL history to have passed for over 300 yards and rush for over 100 yards in the same game.[[178]](#cite_note-189)

### Week 8

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2014_NFL_season&action=edit&section=53 "Edit section: Week 8")]

* [Ben Roethlisberger](/wiki/Ben_Roethlisberger "Ben Roethlisberger") became the first player in NFL history to have two 500-yard passing games.[[179]](#cite_note-190)

### Week 9

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2014_NFL_season&action=edit&section=54 "Edit section: Week 9")]

* [Ben Roethlisberger](/wiki/Ben_Roethlisberger "Ben Roethlisberger") became the first player in NFL history to record at least six touchdown passes in consecutive games.
* The [Denver Broncos](/wiki/2014_Denver_Broncos_season "2014 Denver Broncos season") scored 20 or more points for the 29th consecutive regular-season game, setting a new record. The previous record was held by the [St. Louis Rams](/wiki/St._Louis_Rams "St. Louis Rams") who had 28 such games in [1999](/wiki/1999_St._Louis_Rams_season "1999 St. Louis Rams season") and [2000](/wiki/2000_St._Louis_Rams_season "2000 St. Louis Rams season").[[180]](#cite_note-Week9-191)

### Week 10

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2014_NFL_season&action=edit&section=55 "Edit section: Week 10")]

* [Michael Vick](/wiki/Michael_Vick "Michael Vick") became the first quarterback in NFL history to reach 6,000 career rushing yards.[[181]](#cite_note-192)
* [Julius Thomas](/wiki/Julius_Thomas "Julius Thomas") became the first tight end in NFL history to achieve back-to-back 12-touchdown seasons.
* Julius Thomas tied an NFL record for the most touchdown catches through nine games, with 12, joining [Randy Moss](/wiki/Randy_Moss "Randy Moss") in [2007](/wiki/2007_NFL_season "2007 NFL season").[[182]](#cite_note-193)
* [Aaron Rodgers](/wiki/Aaron_Rodgers "Aaron Rodgers") tied the NFL record with six touchdowns in a half that was set by [Daryle Lamonica](/wiki/Daryle_Lamonica "Daryle Lamonica") in 1969.[[183]](#cite_note-194)

### Week 11

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2014_NFL_season&action=edit&section=56 "Edit section: Week 11")]

* [Adam Vinatieri](/wiki/Adam_Vinatieri "Adam Vinatieri") became the first player in NFL history to score more than 100+ points in 17 different seasons, breaking the record previously held by [Jason Elam](/wiki/Jason_Elam "Jason Elam"), who had 16.[[184]](#cite_note-195)
* [J. J. Watt](/wiki/J._J._Watt "J. J. Watt") became the only player in league history (since sacks became an official statistic) to register a sack, forced fumble, fumble recovery and touchdown reception in the same game.[[185]](#cite_note-196)

### Week 12

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2014_NFL_season&action=edit&section=57 "Edit section: Week 12")]

* The [Atlanta Falcons](/wiki/2014_Atlanta_Falcons_season "2014 Atlanta Falcons season") became the first team in NFL history to lead a division while being three games below .500.[[186]](#cite_note-197)

### Week 15

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2014_NFL_season&action=edit&section=58 "Edit section: Week 15")]

* The [Denver Broncos](/wiki/2014_Denver_Broncos_season "2014 Denver Broncos season") won their 12th consecutive divisional away game, tying an NFL record that the [San Francisco 49ers](/wiki/San_Francisco_49ers "San Francisco 49ers") set from 1987 to 1990;[[187]](#cite_note-198) [John Fox](/wiki/John_Fox_(American_football) "John Fox (American football)") became only the second head coach in NFL history to win four division titles in the first four seasons with a team, joining [Chuck Knox](/wiki/Chuck_Knox "Chuck Knox").[[188]](#cite_note-199)
* The [New England Patriots](/wiki/2014_New_England_Patriots_season "2014 New England Patriots season") set a record of 14 consecutive seasons of winning at least one game against all of their divisional opponents. The previous record of 13 seasons had been set by the [1971](/wiki/1971_Dallas_Cowboys_season "1971 Dallas Cowboys season")–[83 Dallas Cowboys](/wiki/1983_Dallas_Cowboys_season "1983 Dallas Cowboys season").[[189]](#cite_note-200)

### Week 17

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2014_NFL_season&action=edit&section=59 "Edit section: Week 17")]

* [Antonio Gates](/wiki/Antonio_Gates "Antonio Gates") became the fourth tight end in league history to reach 10,000 career receiving yards, joining [Tony Gonzalez](/wiki/Tony_Gonzalez "Tony Gonzalez"), [Jason Witten](/wiki/Jason_Witten "Jason Witten"), and [Shannon Sharpe](/wiki/Shannon_Sharpe "Shannon Sharpe").[[190]](#cite_note-201)
* [J. J. Watt](/wiki/J._J._Watt "J. J. Watt") became the first player in league history to have two separate seasons with 20 or more sacks.[[191]](#cite_note-202)
* [Matt Forte](/wiki/Matt_Forte "Matt Forte") set the league season record for receptions by a running back, finishing with 102.[[192]](#cite_note-203)
* The [Seattle Seahawks](/wiki/2014_Seattle_Seahawks_season "2014 Seattle Seahawks season") became the first team since the 1969–1971 Minnesota Vikings to lead the NFL in fewest points allowed in three consecutive seasons.[[193]](#cite_note-204)
* [Drew Brees](/wiki/Drew_Brees "Drew Brees") tied [Sonny Jurgensen](/wiki/Sonny_Jurgensen "Sonny Jurgensen") and [Dan Marino](/wiki/Dan_Marino "Dan Marino") for the league record for most seasons leading the league in passing yards, with five.[[194]](#cite_note-205)
* [Joe Thomas](/wiki/Joe_Thomas_(offensive_tackle) "Joe Thomas (offensive tackle)") became the first offensive lineman in NFL history to be selected to the Pro Bowl in each of his first eight seasons.
* The [Carolina Panthers](/wiki/2014_Carolina_Panthers_season "2014 Carolina Panthers season") became the second team in the modern era to win their division with a losing record (7–8–1), joining the [2010 Seattle Seahawks](/wiki/2010_Seattle_Seahawks "2010 Seattle Seahawks").

## Stadium changes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2014_NFL_season&action=edit&section=71 "Edit section: Stadium changes")]

* [Levi's Stadium](/wiki/Levi%27s_Stadium "Levi's Stadium"), the new stadium for the [San Francisco 49ers](/wiki/2014_San_Francisco_49ers_season "2014 San Francisco 49ers season"), opened in July 2014.[[267]](#cite_note-278) The stadium is located in [Santa Clara, California](/wiki/Santa_Clara,_California "Santa Clara, California"), directly west of the 49ers long-existing team offices and practice facility, and hosted [Super Bowl 50](/wiki/Super_Bowl_50 "Super Bowl 50") in early 2016.[[268]](#cite_note-279) In November 2013, stadium and team officials requested that the [NFL](/wiki/NFL "NFL") not schedule any weekday home games during the preseason or regular season – including [Monday](/wiki/Monday_Night_Football "Monday Night Football") and [Thursday Night Football](/wiki/Thursday_Night_Football "Thursday Night Football") – due to parking issues within the area. The plan was to borrow parking facilities from nearby businesses and a community college, but the concern was that those entities would have the parking needs on weekdays.[[269]](#cite_note-280) Two months later (January 2014), the Santa Clara City Council approved a two-year deal with the Santa Clara Golf & Tennis Club that would have opened up 10,000 additional parking spaces within walking distance of Levi's Stadium, as well as reimbursed the club [$](/wiki/Dollar "Dollar")250,000 for each year, which also would have enabled the 49ers to host Monday and Thursday night games for both the 2014 and [2015](/wiki/2015_NFL_season "2015 NFL season") seasons.[[270]](#cite_note-281) However, the NFL decided not to schedule the 49ers for any weeknight prime-time games at Levi's Stadium during the 2014 season until traffic flow within the area is figured out.[[271]](#cite_note-282) An exception was made for [Thanksgiving](/wiki/NFL_on_Thanksgiving_Day "NFL on Thanksgiving Day"), when the regular work traffic and parking would not be an issue.[[272]](#cite_note-283)
* Reliant Stadium, the home of the [Houston Texans](/wiki/Houston_Texans "Houston Texans"), was renamed [NRG Stadium](/wiki/NRG_Stadium "NRG Stadium"). Reliant Energy, which held the [naming rights](/wiki/Naming_rights "Naming rights") to the Texans' home field since their inaugural [2002](/wiki/2002_NFL_season "2002 NFL season") season, was previously purchased in 2009 by its parent company [NRG Energy](/wiki/NRG_Energy "NRG Energy").[[273]](#cite_note-284)
* The [Minnesota Vikings](/wiki/2014_Minnesota_Vikings_season "2014 Minnesota Vikings season") played their first of two consecutive seasons at the [University of Minnesota](/wiki/Minnesota_Golden_Gophers_football "Minnesota Golden Gophers football")'s [TCF Bank Stadium](/wiki/TCF_Bank_Stadium "TCF Bank Stadium"), as they awaited the construction of a [new stadium](/wiki/U.S._Bank_Stadium "U.S. Bank Stadium") at the site of the [Hubert H. Humphrey Metrodome](/wiki/Hubert_H._Humphrey_Metrodome "Hubert H. Humphrey Metrodome").[[274]](#cite_note-285) Due to an agreement with the University of Minnesota, the Vikings did not host any Monday or Thursday night games at the stadium. The only exceptions would have been during Thanksgiving (in which the Vikings were not scheduled), Week 16 (which occurs after the university's academic finals), or times when the university is in recess in order to minimize the disruption of on-campus operations, and the Vikings also would have had to plan around the Gophers' home schedule and other UM events, including student move-in week and academic finals.[[275]](#cite_note-286)
* The [Oakland Raiders](/wiki/Oakland_Raiders "Oakland Raiders")' lease on [O.co Coliseum](/wiki/O.co_Coliseum "O.co Coliseum") expired after the [2013](/wiki/2013_NFL_season "2013 NFL season") season. The Raiders played at the stadium for seven of their 2014 home games under a one-year emergency extension but its future after that remained unclear.[[276]](#cite_note-287) Prior to the season, the Raiders had announced their eighth home game was to be played at [Wembley Stadium](/wiki/Wembley_Stadium "Wembley Stadium") as part of the [International Series](/wiki/NFL_International_Series "NFL International Series").[[277]](#cite_note-288)

## Uniforms

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2014_NFL_season&action=edit&section=72 "Edit section: Uniforms")]

* The [Tampa Bay Buccaneers](/wiki/2014_Tampa_Bay_Buccaneers_season "2014 Tampa Bay Buccaneers season") unveiled a new logo and helmet on February 20, 2014,[[278]](#cite_note-289) as well as a new uniform on March 3.[[279]](#cite_note-290) The team also wore a patch with the initials of late owner [Malcolm Glazer](/wiki/Malcolm_Glazer "Malcolm Glazer") who died on May 28 at the age of 85.[[280]](#cite_note-291)
* The [Philadelphia Eagles](/wiki/2014_Philadelphia_Eagles_season "2014 Philadelphia Eagles season") wore their alternate black jerseys during a preseason game against the [New England Patriots](/wiki/2014_New_England_Patriots_season "2014 New England Patriots season") as well as for their Week 6 game vs. the [New York Giants](/wiki/2014_New_York_Giants_season "2014 New York Giants season"), the latter of which marked the first time in franchise history that the team wore all black.[[281]](#cite_note-292) This was the result of the team upgrading their uniforms to take advantage of the "Elite 51" technology from [Nike](/wiki/Nike,_Inc. "Nike, Inc."), which most teams had been using since 2012. The team's standard "midnight green" jerseys did not become available until their Week 10 game vs. the [Carolina Panthers](/wiki/2014_Carolina_Panthers_season "2014 Carolina Panthers season"), because midnight green was considered a custom color and took longer to produce.[[282]](#cite_note-293)[[283]](#cite_note-294)
* The [Detroit Lions](/wiki/2014_Detroit_Lions_season "2014 Detroit Lions season") wore patches with the initials of late owner [William Clay Ford Sr.](/wiki/William_Clay_Ford_Sr. "William Clay Ford Sr.") who died on March 9 at the age of 88.[[284]](#cite_note-295)
* The [Buffalo Bills](/wiki/2014_Buffalo_Bills_season "2014 Buffalo Bills season") wore patches with the initials of late owner [Ralph Wilson](/wiki/Ralph_Wilson "Ralph Wilson") who died on March 25 at the age of 95.[[285]](#cite_note-296)
* The [Tennessee Titans](/wiki/2014_Tennessee_Titans_season "2014 Tennessee Titans season") wore their white jerseys for every game during the 2014 season, for the exception of their Week 8 game vs. the [Houston Texans](/wiki/2014_Houston_Texans_season "2014 Houston Texans season"), in which the Titans wore their alternate navy blue jerseys.[[286]](#cite_note-297) The team did wear their standard light "Titans blue" jerseys for both of their preseason home games. Tommy Smith, who was named the new Titans' president and CEO following the death of longtime owner [Bud Adams](/wiki/Bud_Adams "Bud Adams") in [2013](/wiki/2013_NFL_season "2013 NFL season"), indicated that the light "Titans Blue" jerseys, which had been the team's primary colored jersey since [2008](/wiki/2008_NFL_season "2008 NFL season"), were being phased out. The navy blue jerseys, which were the Titans' primary colored jerseys from 1999 to 2007, returned as the team's primary home jersey beginning in [2015](/wiki/2015_NFL_season "2015 NFL season").[[287]](#cite_note-298)[[288]](#cite_note-299)
* The [Pittsburgh Steelers](/wiki/2014_Pittsburgh_Steelers_season "2014 Pittsburgh Steelers season") honored the 40th anniversary of their first Super Bowl winning team, [Super Bowl IX](/wiki/Super_Bowl_IX "Super Bowl IX"), during their Week 13 game against the [New Orleans Saints](/wiki/2014_New_Orleans_Saints_season "2014 New Orleans Saints season") at [Heinz Field](/wiki/Heinz_Field "Heinz Field") on Sunday, November 30 by wearing a special patch and honored the players at halftime. As it also served as the team's annual [alumni](/wiki/Alumni "Alumni") weekend,[[289]](#cite_note-300) the team wore their standard home uniforms for the game, opting instead to wear their [alternate](/wiki/Third_jersey "Third jersey") [1934 "Bumblebee" throwbacks](/wiki/1934_Pittsburgh_Pirates_(NFL)_season "1934 Pittsburgh Pirates (NFL) season") against the [Indianapolis Colts](/wiki/2014_Indianapolis_Colts_season "2014 Indianapolis Colts season") on October 26.[[290]](#cite_note-301)
* The [New York Giants](/wiki/2014_New_York_Giants_season "2014 New York Giants season") wore a patch commemorating their 90th season.[[291]](#cite_note-302)
* The [Carolina Panthers](/wiki/2014_Carolina_Panthers_season "2014 Carolina Panthers season") and the [Jacksonville Jaguars](/wiki/2014_Jacksonville_Jaguars_season "2014 Jacksonville Jaguars season") wore patches to commemorate the 20th season of play for both franchises.[[292]](#cite_note-303)

## Media

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2014_NFL_season&action=edit&section=73 "Edit section: Media")]

2014 was the first season under a nine-year television contract with [CBS](/wiki/NFL_on_CBS "NFL on CBS") (almost all AFC afternoon away games), [Fox](/wiki/NFL_on_Fox "NFL on Fox") (almost all NFC afternoon away games), [NBC](/wiki/NBC_Sunday_Night_Football "NBC Sunday Night Football") ([Sunday Night Football](/wiki/NBC_Sunday_Night_Football "NBC Sunday Night Football") games, Thanksgiving night game, and the Kickoff game); and an eight-year contract with [ESPN](/wiki/ESPN "ESPN") ([Monday Night Football](/wiki/Monday_Night_Football "Monday Night Football") games).[[293]](#cite_note-Multichannel-304)[[294]](#cite_note-305) Among the changes from the previous television contracts were allowing games to be "cross-flexed" between CBS and Fox, enabling CBS to televise NFC away games, and Fox to broadcast AFC away games (*[see above](#Changes_to_flex_scheduling))*. NBC was given the rights to broadcast a Divisional playoff game, and their [Spanish language](/wiki/Spanish_language "Spanish language") sister network [Mun2](/wiki/Mun2 "Mun2") simulcasted select NBC games, with Spanish language graphics and play-by-play during the Thanksgiving game; Mun2 changed their branding to the more sports-centric NBC Universo with their Spanish simulcast of Super Bowl XLIX. The contract also allowed ESPN to televise a Wild Card playoff game (which, like other NFL games carried on cable, was simulcast on an over-the-air station in each of the team's primary market).[[293]](#cite_note-Multichannel-304) In addition, ESPN began exclusively televising all Pro Bowls for the next eight seasons.

Under a new, one-year contract, CBS also took over the production of *[Thursday Night Football](/wiki/Thursday_Night_Football "Thursday Night Football")* and aired the first eight games of the package, plus half of a Week 16 Saturday doubleheader, in simulcast with [NFL Network](/wiki/NFL_Network "NFL Network"). The arrangement was meant to bring more prominence and higher production values to *TNF*, which had historically hosted the league's least-watched primetime games.[[295]](#cite_note-sbj-tnf-306)[[296]](#cite_note-usatoday-nflcbs-307) As part of the arrangement, CBS affiliates were given the [right of first refusal](/wiki/Right_of_first_refusal "Right of first refusal") to air the required local simulcasts of *TNF* games solely broadcast elsewhere by NFL Network.[[69]](#cite_note-cbj-nflncbsgames-69)[[297]](#cite_note-cincy-wlwttnf-308) The agreement was renewed for the 2015 season.[[298]](#cite_note-tnfvariety-309)

CBS made several personnel changes following the retirements of [Don Criqui](/wiki/Don_Criqui "Don Criqui"), [Marv Albert](/wiki/Marv_Albert "Marv Albert"), and [Dan Dierdorf](/wiki/Dan_Dierdorf "Dan Dierdorf") from the NFL broadcast booth. [Brian Anderson](/wiki/Brian_Anderson_(broadcaster) "Brian Anderson (broadcaster)") and [Tom McCarthy](/wiki/Tom_McCarthy_(broadcaster) "Tom McCarthy (broadcaster)") joined as play-by-play announcers, while [Chris Simms](/wiki/Chris_Simms "Chris Simms") (Phil Simms' son) and [Trent Green](/wiki/Trent_Green "Trent Green") joined as color commentators, both of whom come over from Fox Sports. CBS used a three-man announcing crew of [Andrew Catalon](/wiki/Andrew_Catalon "Andrew Catalon"), [Steve Beuerlein](/wiki/Steve_Beuerlein "Steve Beuerlein") and [Steve Tasker](/wiki/Steve_Tasker "Steve Tasker") on select regional NFL games, a departure from the typical practice of reserving three-man crews for national and high-profile contests.[[299]](#cite_note-310) [Mike Carey](/wiki/Mike_Carey_(American_football) "Mike Carey (American football)") joined the broadcast team as the network's in-house rules expert.[[300]](#cite_note-311) [Jim Nantz](/wiki/Jim_Nantz "Jim Nantz") and [Phil Simms](/wiki/Phil_Simms "Phil Simms") handled play-by-play for the *Thursday Night Football* games.[[295]](#cite_note-sbj-tnf-306) There were also changes to *[The NFL Today](/wiki/The_NFL_Today "The NFL Today")*, as [Shannon Sharpe](/wiki/Shannon_Sharpe "Shannon Sharpe") and [Dan Marino](/wiki/Dan_Marino "Dan Marino") both left the panel, replaced by [Bart Scott](/wiki/Bart_Scott "Bart Scott") and [Tony Gonzalez](/wiki/Tony_Gonzalez "Tony Gonzalez").[[301]](#cite_note-312)

On Fox, the most notable personnel change was the demotion of [Pam Oliver](/wiki/Pam_Oliver "Pam Oliver"), Fox's top sideline reporter for nearly 20 years, to the second broadcast team as [Erin Andrews](/wiki/Erin_Andrews "Erin Andrews") took over the spot on the first broadcast team.[[302]](#cite_note-313) Other personnel changes include the additions of [Donovan McNabb](/wiki/Donovan_McNabb "Donovan McNabb"), [David Diehl](/wiki/David_Diehl "David Diehl"), [Kirk Morrison](/wiki/Kirk_Morrison "Kirk Morrison"), and [Brendan Ayanbadejo](/wiki/Brendan_Ayanbadejo "Brendan Ayanbadejo") to Fox's stable of color commentators, replacing outgoing commentators Tom McCarthy, [Brian Billick](/wiki/Brian_Billick "Brian Billick") and [Tim Ryan](/wiki/Tim_Ryan_(American_football,_born_1967) "Tim Ryan (American football, born 1967)").[[303]](#cite_note-314) [Brady Quinn](/wiki/Brady_Quinn "Brady Quinn") also joined the Fox stable. Also briefly joining Fox's play-by-play stable was [UFC](/wiki/UFC "UFC") announcer [Mike Goldberg](/wiki/Mike_Goldberg "Mike Goldberg"); Goldberg's time doing NFL telecasts was cut short after one game, and he was replaced by [Tim Brando](/wiki/Tim_Brando "Tim Brando"), who came over from CBS.[[304]](#cite_note-315)
